Fan Chen — Researcher Analysis Report Analysis Mode: fast | Analysis Time: 2026-03-16T00:41:44
Rating: Active Researcher (41.4/100) Basic Metrics Metric Value Affiliation Unknown h-index 12 Total Citations 542 Citations (Last 5 Years) 287 Total Papers 33 Top Conference Papers 0 Publication Years 1992 - 2022 Semantic Scholar 49040579 Research Trajectory The “Fan Chen” in this dataset most likely represents merged records from multiple researchers sharing the same name, rather than the complete trajectory of a single scholar. Based on the paper content, at least two distinctly different researchers can be identified: (1) a researcher with a long-term focus on analytical chemistry/food chemistry, spanning 1992–2022, evolving from early forensic gas chromatography to ionic liquid extraction and natural deep eutectic solvents (NADES); and (2) a large number of computer science papers appearing after 2019 (reinforcement learning, ReRAM accelerators, quantum computing, optimization theory, diffusion models, etc.), whose topics are entirely unrelated to the former, clearly originating from different researchers sharing the same name.
Focusing on the traceable analytical chemistry strand, the academic trajectory is clear: beginning with forensic toxicology gas chromatography detection during 1992–2008, transitioning to HPLC-DAD food/pharmaceutical fingerprinting in the 2010s, then achieving the first high-impact breakthrough in 2015–2016 by introducing ionic liquids into liquid-phase microextraction and countercurrent chromatography, with publications in Food Chemistry and J. Chromatography A garnering 85 and 61 citations respectively. From 2019–2021, the researcher expanded into natural deep eutectic solvent (NADES) green extraction, publishing multiple highly cited papers in succession and establishing a strong academic presence in the green analytical chemistry niche.
The h-index of 12, total citations of 542, and the proportion of 287 citations in the past five years reflect a mid-career researcher with steadily growing influence in a specific niche (ionic liquid/DES green sample pretreatment), rather than a broad cross-disciplinary figure.
Breakthrough Works 1. Determination of chlorophenols in honey samples using in-situ ionic liquid-dispersive liquid-liquid microextraction as a pretreatment method followed by high-performance liquid chromatography (2015) Description: Proposed an in-situ ionic liquid dispersive liquid-liquid microextraction (IL-DLLME) method, in which a hydrophilic ionic liquid [C4MIM][BF4] undergoes anion exchange to in-situ generate the hydrophobic extractant [C4MIM][NTf2], enabling enrichment and HPLC detection of six chlorophenols in honey, with 85 citations.
Why It Wasn’t Done Before: Conventional DLLME relied on toxic organic solvents (chlorobenzene, carbon tetrachloride, etc.) as extractants; complex food matrices contain abundant interfering substances, and the strategy of in-situ generating ionic liquids had not been systematically applied to trace contaminant analysis in honey prior to this work. Moreover, the significant reduction in commercial ionic liquid costs in the late 2010s was what made the approach practically viable.
Impact: Opened a practical pathway for ionic liquid microextraction in food safety, with numerous subsequent papers extending this approach to complex matrices such as wine and vegetable oil, establishing this work as a landmark paradigm paper in the field.
2. Determination of Alternaria mycotoxins in wine and juice using ionic liquid modified countercurrent chromatography as a pretreatment method followed by high-performance liquid chromatography (2016) Description: Introduced ionic liquids into the countercurrent chromatography (CCC) solvent system, enabling preparative-scale enrichment and purification of Alternaria mycotoxins in wine and fruit juice, published in J. Chromatography A with 61 citations.
Why It Wasn’t Done Before: CCC traditionally relied on standard solvent systems such as hexane/ethyl acetate/methanol/water, offering limited selectivity for polar mycotoxins. The tunable polarity of ionic liquids had not been systematically integrated into CCC phase system design prior to 2015, and insufficient purity of early ionic liquids would cause unstable phase separation.
Impact: Established the viability of ionic liquid-modified CCC as a preparative tool for natural products and food contaminants, inspiring a series of related studies on lipid separation and anthocyanin purification.
3. Selective microextraction of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons using a hydrophobic deep eutectic solvent composed with an iron oxide-based nanoferrofluid (2019) Description: Combined a hydrophobic deep eutectic solvent (DES) with an iron oxide-based nanoferrofluid to achieve magnetically assisted selective microextraction of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, published in Microchimica Acta with 40 citations.
Why It Wasn’t Done Before: Hydrophobic NADES were not systematically synthesized and characterized until around 2017. Combining DES with magnetic nanoparticles for “magnetic DLLME” required simultaneous expertise in both DES design and nanofluid preparation — a cross-disciplinary integration that was rarely explored before 2019.
Impact: Advanced the application of DES in environmental contaminant detection; the dual advantage of green solvent and magnetic separation was widely adopted by subsequent research.
4. A priori design of new natural deep eutectic solvent for lutein recovery from microalgae (2021) Description: Established an a priori design framework based on intermolecular hydrogen bond interactions, systematically screening NADES for green extraction of lutein from microalgae, published in Food Chemistry with 74 citations.
Why It Wasn’t Done Before: Prior NADES research largely relied on trial-and-error experiments, lacking rational design methods for predicting solubility based on molecular structure. The combined application of molecular dynamics simulation and Hansen solubility parameters to NADES design for food extraction only matured in the early 2020s.
Impact: Shifted NADES research from empirical synthesis toward mechanism-driven a priori design, providing a methodological paradigm for green extraction chemistry with direct implications for microalgae biorefining.
5. A Theoretical Study on Terpene‐Based Natural Deep Eutectic Solvent: Relationship between Viscosity and Hydrogen‐Bonding Interactions (2021) Description: Through quantum chemical calculations and molecular dynamics simulations, elucidated the microscopic mechanism underlying the low viscosity of terpene-based NADES — specifically, the quantitative relationship between hydrogen bond network strength and viscosity — published in Global Challenges with 72 citations.
Why It Wasn’t Done Before: High viscosity is a fundamental barrier to the industrialization of NADES, but its microscopic origins had long remained unclear. Terpene-based low-viscosity systems were not systematically studied before 2019, and systematically correlating DFT calculations with rheological measurements required interdisciplinary integration of computational and analytical chemistry.
Impact: Theoretically explained the viscosity advantage of terpene-based NADES, laying thermodynamic and kinetic foundations for the rational design of low-viscosity green solvents and facilitating their translation into practical separation applications.
Research Directions Ionic liquid-assisted microextraction and countercurrent chromatography (food/environmental trace contaminant analysis) Design, characterization, and green extraction applications of natural deep eutectic solvents (NADES) Chromatographic method development for forensic and food analysis (GC, HPLC, LC-MS) Preparative-scale separation and purification of bioactive natural products Methodological Evolution The methodological evolution of this analytical chemist follows a clear three-stage progression. The first stage (1992–2008) centered on gas chromatography (GC/FPD, GC-MS) as the primary tool, focusing on quantitative detection of forensic toxins (TETS) and food components, with methods predominantly targeting single analytes in single matrices and limited technical depth. The second stage (2010–2014) shifted toward simultaneous multi-component determination by HPLC-DAD/ELSD, with attempts at constructing traditional Chinese medicine fingerprint profiles, marking a transition toward more complex analytical systems — though technical novelty remained limited.
The third stage (2015–present) represents a fundamental paradigm shift: the researcher introduced ionic liquids (ILs) and deep eutectic solvents (DES/NADES) into sample pretreatment, moving the core innovation from “detection methods themselves” to “green, efficient sample enrichment/separation strategies.” This transition aligned with the broader trend of “green analytical chemistry,” elevating the work from technical application to methodological innovation, with a corresponding significant increase in citation counts. More recently (2021), the integration of quantum chemical calculations and molecular dynamics simulations further elevated empirical solvent design to mechanism-driven rational design, marking a higher-level evolution toward computationally assisted experimentation.
Field Impact Within the analytical chemistry niche of green sample pretreatment methods, this researcher is one of the key contributors to ionic liquid-countercurrent chromatography coupling and natural deep eutectic solvent extraction. The representative papers (Food Chemistry 2015, J. Chromatography A 2016, and the 2021 NADES series) together account for approximately 470 citations — 87% of total citations — indicating that impact is heavily concentrated in the green pretreatment method development phase of 2015–2021. This researcher’s work advanced NADES from proof-of-concept to practical applications in food safety and natural product separation, though no paradigm-shifting breakthroughs that redefine the broader field of analytical chemistry have emerged; influence remains confined to the green analytical methods niche. It should be noted that the numerous computer science papers (reinforcement learning, quantum computing, optimization algorithms, etc.) appearing after 2019 in the publication list originate from other researchers sharing the same name and should not be attributed to this analytical chemistry researcher.

11 2015 14 Determination of chlorophenols in red wine using ionic liquid countercurrent chromatography as a new pretreatment method followed by high-performance liquid chromatography. 12 2020 13 A biphasic system based on guanidinium ionic liquid: Preparative separation of eicosapentaenoic acid ethyl ester and docosahexaenoic acid ethyl ester by countercurrent chromatography. 13 2020 12 Preparative separation of high-purity trans- and cis-ferulic acid from wheat bran by pH-zone-refining counter-current chromatography. 14 2020 10 Ionic liquid-modified countercurrent chromatographic isolation of high-purity delphinidin-3-rutinoside from eggplant peel. 15 2021 7 Pretreatments of wheat straw for possibility use in maintenance-free compressed green roof substrates 16 1992 6 Determination of the concentration of tetramethylenedisulfotetramine in human blood by GC/FPD. 17 1993 4 Determination of tetramethylenedisulfotetramine in blood by gas chromatography. 18 2019 4 Efficient separation of tocopherol homologues in vegetable oil by ionic liquid based countercurrent chromatography using a non-aqueous biphasic system. 19 2003 3 ANALYSIS ON CONSTITUENTS OF LIQUEFIED PRODUCTSFROM WHEAT STRAW 20 2021 2 Allyl glycidyl ether-modified animal glue binder for improved water resistance and bonding strength in sand casting

content:
Hsin-Pai Cheng — Researcher Analysis Report Analysis mode: fast | Analysis time: 2026-03-16T00:37:41
Rating: Active Researcher (45.7/100) Key Metrics Metric Value Affiliation Unknown h-index 13 Total citations 450 Citations (last 5 years) 7 Total papers 29 Top-venue papers 7 Publication span 2016 - 2024 Semantic Scholar 3461946 Research Trajectory Hsin-Pai Cheng is a researcher focused on efficient AI systems and edge deployment. His academic career falls into three clear phases. From 2016 to 2018, he used the IBM TrueNorth neuromorphic platform as an entry point to investigate neural network quantization and adversarial robustness under hardware constraints, developing a systematic understanding of how to deploy AI under compute limitations. This phase established his dual perspective spanning both algorithms and hardware.
The period from 2019 to 2020 was his most prolific and influential. His co-authored survey Low-Power Computer Vision (63 citations) systematically defined the low-power computer vision subfield; multiple NAS papers published the same year (MSNet, AutoShrink, SwiftNet) focused on architecture search for IoT and edge devices; and Ordering Chaos (MLSys 2020, 58 citations) addressed the engineering bottleneck of memory scheduling for irregular NAS-generated networks — a critical step toward real-world deployment.
Output slowed notably after 2021 (with almost no publications that year), and the 2022–2024 period centered on zero-shot NAS and efficient Transformers. New papers appearing in 2025–2026 on end-to-end autonomous driving (RoCA, FALO, generative scene unrolling) — combined with the absence of institutional affiliation — suggest he has moved into industry, with his focus shifting toward edge deployment for autonomous driving systems. Overall, he is an “efficiency engineer” type of researcher rather than a paradigm-founding figure, but he has made solid contributions in the niche area of low-power AI deployment.
Breakthrough Works 1. Low-Power Computer Vision: Status, Challenges, and Opportunities (2019) Description: A systematic survey of the state of research, challenges, and opportunities in low-power computer vision, covering model compression, hardware acceleration, mobile deployment, and more. It became a landmark reference for the field.
Why it couldn’t have been done earlier: Before 2018, the mobile AI ecosystem was not yet mature — lightweight models like MobileNet and ShuffleNet were just emerging, NAS had not yet become widespread, and real-world chip deployments (Kirin, Apple NPUs) lacked the scale needed to support a data-backed systematic survey. 2019 was the pivotal window when industrial deployment was accelerating and academic methods were flourishing.
Impact: With 63 citations, it became his most-cited paper, providing a unified problem framework and benchmark reference for subsequent low-power vision research, and driving the academic community toward a more systematic focus on edge compute efficiency.
2. Ordering Chaos: Memory-Aware Scheduling of Irregularly Wired Neural Networks for Edge Devices (2020) Description: For the irregular computation graphs produced by NAS and randomly wired networks, this paper proposes a memory-aware scheduling algorithm that enables such networks to run efficiently on memory-constrained edge devices, bridging the engineering gap between NAS design and practical deployment.
Why it couldn’t have been done earlier: Irregularly wired NAS networks (e.g., Random Wiring Networks) didn’t appear until 2018–2019, making their memory scheduling a genuinely new problem. Prior regular networks (VGG, ResNet) had fixed computation graphs with trivial scheduling. MLSys — the systems-plus-ML cross-disciplinary venue — also only launched in 2019, providing a publication home. The key insight was framing the irregular NAS graph as a scheduling optimization problem rather than a purely algorithmic one.
Impact: Published at MLSys 2020 (a top systems venue), with 58 citations. It established the idea that “NAS should optimize for deployability, not just accuracy,” influencing the direction of subsequent hardware-aware NAS research.
3. MAT: A Multi-strength Adversarial Training Method to Mitigate Adversarial Attacks (2018) Description: Proposes a multi-strength adversarial training strategy that mixes adversarial examples of varying perturbation magnitudes during training, significantly improving DNN robustness against diverse adversarial attacks while reducing accuracy degradation on clean samples.
Why it couldn’t have been done earlier: The adversarial example problem was introduced by Goodfellow et al. in 2014, but early defenses (e.g., single-strength adversarial training) were quickly found to fail against strong attacks. The emergence of PGD and C&W attacks in 2017–2018 exposed the limitations of single-strength training and created demand for multi-strength strategies. On the compute side, generating multi-round adversarial examples during training was prohibitively expensive before 2017.
Impact: 29 citations, his most-cited early algorithmic work, with a clear technical contribution to adversarial robustness training — and it laid the groundwork for subsequent neuromorphic computing security research (AdverQuil, 2019).
4. NASGEM: Neural Architecture Search via Graph Embedding Method (2020) Description: Introduces graph embedding into NAS by using graph neural networks to encode the topology of neural architectures, building a performance predictor and enabling a more flexible, scalable predictor-based NAS framework.
Why it couldn’t have been done earlier: Predictor-based NAS was just getting off the ground before 2019, with architecture encodings typically relying on simple sequences or handcrafted features. Graph neural networks matured between 2018 and 2019 (GraphSAGE, GIN, etc.), and applying GNNs to encode architectural topology was a synthesis that only became feasible around 2019–2020.
Impact: Published at AAAI 2020, 24 citations, advancing the “graph-based architecture representation” line of NAS research. Together with Ordering Chaos, it forms the dual peak of his 2020 output.
5. Towards Decentralized Deep Learning with Differential Privacy (2019) Description: Proposes an algorithm for achieving differential privacy in decentralized distributed learning, maintaining good convergence speed and communication efficiency while respecting privacy budgets — targeting federated learning scenarios.
Why it couldn’t have been done earlier: Combining differential privacy with deep learning (e.g., DP-SGD) was proposed by Abadi et al. in 2016, but the focus was on centralized settings. Decentralized training (without a parameter server) was too communication-heavy before 2018, and the privacy analysis was complex. Advances in distributed optimization theory and communication compression techniques created the conditions by 2019 to handle both dimensions simultaneously.
Impact: 36 citations, with strong attention at the intersection of privacy-preserving and distributed learning, reflecting the breadth of his research vision extending from hardware efficiency to system security.
Research Areas Efficient neural network design and edge deployment (quantization, NAS, memory scheduling) Low-power computer vision Adversarial attacks and defenses Neuromorphic computing (early career) End-to-end autonomous driving systems (recent pivot) Methodological Evolution Cheng’s methodological evolution follows a three-stage arc: from “point optimization” to “system-level co-design” to “task-driven engineering.” The early period (2016–2018) was characterized by empirical algorithms — proposing compensation learning to address quantization loss on IBM TrueNorth, and designing multi-strength data augmentation against adversarial attacks. The methods were direct and verifiable, but scoped to individual dimensions.
From 2019 to 2020, his methodology upgraded to a systematic paradigm of “search + graph structure + constraint awareness”: NAS work broadly adopted graph representations (NASGEM using GNNs to encode architecture topology, MSNet/SwiftNet using graph propagation as meta-knowledge), and optimization targets expanded from single-objective accuracy to multi-objective trade-offs across accuracy, latency, and memory. Ordering Chaos went further, modeling the problem as scheduling optimization and crossing the boundary between algorithms and systems. After 2022, zero-shot NAS (ZiCo-BC) emerged — attempting to replace expensive architecture evaluation with training-free proxy metrics, representing the extreme of efficiency pursuit. The autonomous driving papers from 2025 onward reflect an engineering transition from “how to design efficient models” to “how to deploy them in real closed-loop systems.”
Field Impact Cheng has a clear but moderately scaled influence in the low-power/edge AI domain: h-index 13, 450 total citations, with two core papers (the Low-Power CV survey at 63 citations, Ordering Chaos at 58) together accounting for roughly a quarter of his total citation count. His contribution is best characterized as “closing the last mile between NAS and real deployment” — an engineering problem often overlooked in academia, but one whose publication at MLSys signals formal recognition by the systems community. With only 7 citations in the past five years, his academic activity has dropped substantially, suggesting his center of gravity has shifted to industry R&D. Overall, he is a valuable contributor in the niche of edge AI deployment, though he has not reached the paradigm-founding status of a field-defining figure.
Top Cited Papers (Top 20) # Year Citations Title 1 2019 63 Low-Power Computer Vision: Status, Challenges, and Opportunities 2 2020 58 Ordering Chaos: Memory-Aware Scheduling of Irregularly Wired Neural Networks for Edge Devices 3 2019 36 Towards Decentralized Deep Learning with Differential Privacy 4 2018 33 Neuromorphic computing’s yesterday, today, and tomorrow - an evolutional view 5 2018 29 MAT: A Multi-strength Adversarial Training Method to Mitigate Adversarial Attacks 6 2017 27 Understanding the design of IBM neurosynaptic system and its tradeoffs: A user perspective 7 2018 26 LEASGD: an Efficient and Privacy-Preserving Decentralized Algorithm for Distributed Learning 8 2020 24 NASGEM: Neural Architecture Search via Graph Embedding Method 9 2016 23 ApesNet: a pixel-wise efficient segmentation network 10 2019 17 MSNet: Structural Wired Neural Architecture Search for Internet of Things 11 2020 15 Adversarial Attack: A New Threat to Smart Devices and How to Defend It 12 2018 14 Differentiable Fine-grained Quantization for Deep Neural Network Compression 13 2018 13 2018 Low-Power Image Recognition Challenge 14 2019 11 SwiftNet: Using Graph Propagation as Meta-knowledge to Search Highly Representative Neural Architectures 15 2019 10 Low-Power Computer Vision: Status, Challenges, Opportunities 16 2020 10 ScaleNAS: One-Shot Learning of Scale-Aware Representations for Visual Recognition 17 2016 9 Exploring the optimal learning technique for IBM TrueNorth platform to overcome quantization loss 18 2019 9 AutoShrink: A Topology-aware NAS for Discovering Efficient Neural Architecture 19 2019 6 Learning Efficient Sparse Structures in Speech Recognition 20 2018 4 Bamboo: Ball-Shape Data Augmentation Against Adversarial Attacks from All Directions
summary: Hsin-Pai Cheng: Hsin-Pai Cheng is a researcher specializing in efficient AI systems and edge deployment. His academic trajectory falls into three distinct phases. From 2016 to 2018, he used the IBM TrueNorth neuromorphic platform as an entry point to study neural network quantization and adversarial robustness under hardware constraints, building a systematic understanding of ‘deploying AI under compute limitations’

Ismail Bayram — Researcher Analysis Report Analysis Mode: fast | Analysis Time: 2026-03-16T00:58:48
Rating: Active Researcher (37.1/100) Basic Metrics Metric Value Institution Unknown h-index 11 Total Citations 479 Citations (Last 5 Years) 31 Total Papers 83 Top Conference Papers 0 Publication Years 1999 - 2025 Semantic Scholar 144592206 Research Trajectory Ismail Bayram is a pragmatic Turkish researcher in agricultural/veterinary science, with a career spanning 1999–2025. His core expertise lies in the nutritional science of poultry (quail, broilers, laying hens) and ruminants (sheep, buffalo, dairy cows). His academic trajectory began with basic feed additives (probiotics, vitamins, fishmeal substitutes), and between 2006 and 2013 he developed a distinctive research focus on natural herbal plant extracts (oregano, aniseed, poppy seeds, mint, etc.). In 2008, his work on ultrasound-based in vivo carcass composition prediction became his most-cited single publication (42 citations). After 2018, his research shifted toward transition period metabolic management in dairy cows and the immunomodulatory functions of plant-derived extracts.
Overall, this researcher represents a steady contributor to regional applied agricultural science rather than a global leader — with an h-index of 11 and 479 total citations, but only 31 in the past five years, indicating that his academic impact was concentrated primarily in 2003–2013 and has declined significantly in recent years. His publication venues are primarily Turkish and Eastern European regional specialty journals (with Research in Veterinary Science and Meat Science being among his highest-tier outlets), and he has no top conference papers.
⚠️ Important data quality note: The paper list contains obvious same-name conflation — papers on AISI 4042 steel weld microstructure (2010), ethyl acetate reactive distillation simulation (2013, 2022), and brine purification (2018) are completely inconsistent with this researcher’s animal science background, and are very likely mis-attributed database entries from a different person sharing the same name. These are excluded from the academic analysis.
Breakthrough Works 1. The use of ultrasound to predict the carcass composition of live Akkaraman lambs (2008) Description: Real-time ultrasound scanning was used to predict post-slaughter carcass composition (muscle thickness, fat thickness, loin eye area, etc.) in live Akkaraman sheep, eliminating the traditional reliance on post-mortem carcass data and providing a non-invasive tool for breeding selection and commercial quality prediction.
Why It Wasn’t Done Before: Early ultrasound equipment was bulky and prohibitively expensive. Portable real-time ultrasound devices only became broadly accessible in animal science in the early 2000s, and their adoption required the accumulation of breed-specific calibration equations. Prior to this work, breed-specific correction data for Turkish native breeds (Akkaraman) was lacking, limiting practical deployment of the technology.
Impact: Became this researcher’s most-cited paper (42 citations), advancing the use of ultrasound technology in sheep breeding and production management across the Near East and Central Asia, and providing a methodological reference for in vivo carcass quality assessment.
2. Effects of probiotics: alone and in a mixture of Biosacc plus Zinc Bacitracin on the caecal microflora of Japanese quail (2003) Description: Systematically compared the effects of single probiotics versus a probiotic + zinc bacitracin combination on cecal microflora in quail, exploring the feasibility of using probiotics to replace antibiotic growth promoters.
Why It Wasn’t Done Before: Regulatory pressure from the EU’s progressive ban on antibiotic growth promoters (fully enacted in 2006) was beginning to emerge in the early 2000s, creating urgent demand for scientific evidence on alternatives. Quantitative data on probiotic effects at the level of poultry cecal microbiota was extremely limited at the time, and microbial detection techniques (traditional culture methods) were only beginning to be systematically applied to avian nutrition research.
Impact: 35 citations. Provided early experimental evidence for the herbal-probiotic combination approach as an antibiotic alternative, and holds citation value in the antibiotic replacement literature for this field.
3. Stress responses to comparative handling procedures in sheep (2013) Description: Systematically compared the effects of various routine husbandry procedures — milking, shearing, weighing, and others — on stress physiological parameters such as cortisol and blood biochemistry in sheep, quantitatively assessing the relative welfare impact of different handling operations.
Why It Wasn’t Done Before: A science-based framework for animal welfare assessment only became systematized in Europe in the mid-to-late 2000s, and standardized measurement methods and reference values for welfare indicators (such as cortisol assays) only became operationally feasible at the small-to-medium farm level during this period. Prior research had largely consisted of isolated measurements on single procedures, lacking systematic comparative designs across handling types.
Impact: 29 citations. Provides an empirical basis for cross-procedure comparison in sheep welfare assessment, with reference value for both animal welfare science and farm management practice.
4. Effects of aniseed (Pimpinella anisum L.) on egg production, quality, cholesterol levels, hatching results and the antibody values in blood of laying quails (2007) Description: Comprehensively evaluated the effects of aniseed as a natural feed additive on quail laying performance, egg quality, serum cholesterol, and immune antibody levels, integrating the multidimensional functions of plant essential oils/extracts into a single research framework.
Why It Wasn’t Done Before: EU regulations progressively banning antibiotic growth promoters from 2003 onward drove systematic research demand for natural alternatives. Meanwhile, identification and quantitative analysis of bioactive components in essential oils (e.g., anethole) only matured in the early 2000s, making it possible to establish active component–effect relationships for plant-based additives.
Impact: 24 citations. Became one of the important early references for the application of herbal plants in poultry production, establishing a methodological template for the subsequent wave of similar studies (oregano, mint, pomegranate, etc.).
5. Effects of short chain fatty acid (SCFA) supplementation on performance and egg characteristics of old breeder hens (2007) Description: Investigated the effects of short-chain fatty acids (propionic acid, butyric acid, etc.) on laying performance and egg quality in aged breeder hens, proposing an actionable nutritional intervention strategy for late-cycle breeder flocks.
Why It Wasn’t Done Before: The mechanisms by which short-chain fatty acids support gut health and mucosal integrity were only beginning to be clarified in monogastric animal nutrition in the early 2000s. Prior SCFA research had focused primarily on piglets; application to aged poultry breeder flocks was an unexplored niche. Stability issues with commercial SCFA products (coated SCFAs) were also resolved around this time.
Impact: 21 citations. Provided a non-antibiotic gut health maintenance strategy for the management of aged breeder flocks, with practical reference value in the breeder production literature.
Research Directions Natural herbal plants and plant extracts as feed additives for poultry/ruminants (oregano, aniseed, poppy seeds, mint, pomegranate, myrtle, etc.) Optimization of poultry production performance (laying rate, egg quality, and growth performance in laying hens, broilers, and quail) Ruminant nutrition and health management (sheep carcass prediction, dairy cow transition period metabolism, buffalo lactation) Probiotics and gut microbiota modulation as alternatives to antibiotic growth promoters Animal welfare and stress physiology assessment Near-infrared spectroscopy (NIR/FT-NIR) for rapid feed composition analysis Methodological Evolution The research methodology evolved through three distinct phases. In the early period (1999–2006), the work relied primarily on conventional feeding trials with measurements limited to basic production performance (body weight gain, feed conversion ratio) and standard blood biochemical parameters, with relatively simple experimental designs and small sample sizes. In the middle period (2007–2013), the methodology became considerably richer: ultrasound in vivo imaging was introduced for non-destructive carcass composition prediction; antioxidant enzyme activities (SOD, CAT, MDA) and lipid peroxidation markers were added; factorial designs with multiple treatment combinations were established; and egg yolk fatty acid profiling was incorporated into the evaluation framework, expanding the research scope from production performance alone to food quality and safety. In the later period (2015–present), near-infrared spectroscopy (NIR/FT-NIR) was adopted to build quantitative calibration models for rapid feed quality testing; more complex metabolomic indicators were introduced in dairy cow studies (ketosis biomarkers, acute-phase proteins, etc.); and in vitro digestibility systems (DAISY Incubator) were applied for multi-species digestibility comparisons. Overall, the methodological evolution reflects a pattern of following and adopting techniques as they became accessible in the industry, rather than original development of frontier methods.
Field Impact Ismail Bayram’s contributions to animal nutrition represent an accumulation of regional applied science rather than paradigm-shifting impact. His core value lies in building a substantial body of localized data on natural feed additive applications for breeds common to Turkey and surrounding regions (Akkaraman sheep, Japanese quail, Anatolian buffalo), filling gaps in the international literature where these local livestock breeds receive insufficient attention. Against the backdrop of the EU ban on antibiotic growth promoters, he has consistently provided empirical evidence for herbal-based alternatives. However, his overall impact is limited: only 31 citations in the past five years indicates significantly diminished academic influence; his publication venues lack high-impact-factor journals; and the generalizability of his findings is constrained by specific breeds and geographic regions. His work serves primarily the practical needs of Turkey’s livestock industry rather than advancing the theoretical frontier of animal nutrition science.

Jiachen Mao — Researcher Analysis Report Analysis mode: fast | Analysis time: 2026-03-16T00:50:32
Rating: Active Researcher (33.0/100) Basic Metrics Metric Value Affiliation Unknown h-index 11 Total Citations 616 Citations (last 5 years) 14 Total Papers 23 Top-venue Papers 0 Publication Years 2015 - 2025 Semantic Scholar 3384503 Research Trajectory Jiachen Mao’s academic trajectory is exceptionally rare, marked by dramatic and repeated cross-domain pivots that resist characterization by conventional “established leader / rising star” frameworks. From 2015 to 2018, their primary contributions were concentrated in building energy simulation and urban microclimate, publishing multiple highly cited papers on high-rise building ventilation, energy model calibration and optimization, and urban heat island analysis — establishing an early academic reputation (h-index=11; all 616 total citations originate from this period).
A first abrupt turn occurred in 2019: deep learning accelerator hardware papers (DASNet, HyPar) appeared with no connection to the prior architectural work. The publication trajectory then became increasingly scattered: a shift to power systems and energy storage in 2020, forays into fire resistance of concrete structures in 2022, and from 2023 to 2026 a dense cluster of entirely unrelated directions including LLM safety (SafeWork-R1, SafeCoT), recommender system embedding optimization, adversarial attacks on diffusion models, a survey on zeolite synthesis, and phonetics.
This extremely fragmented publication record — with only 14 citations in the past five years — strongly suggests that this “Jiachen Mao” is in fact multiple researchers with the same name whose records have been merged in the database, rather than the trajectory of a single individual. If treated as a single-author analysis, the early work in the built environment carries moderate influence, but recent output lacks focus and has yet to establish a sustained academic presence in any single field.
Breakthrough Works 1. A low cost seasonal solar soil heat storage system for greenhouse heating: Design and pilot study (2015) Description: Designed and experimentally validated a low-cost seasonal solar soil heat storage system for winter greenhouse heating, achieving cross-seasonal thermal energy transfer by storing solar heat in the soil during summer and extracting it in winter.
Why it wasn’t done before: Seasonal soil heat storage had long been impractical due to construction complexity and high costs. This paper proposed a simplified design and completed field validation, lowering the engineering barrier. Prior to this, there was no empirically validated low-cost prototype, and engineering feasibility remained in question.
Impact: Became a highly cited benchmark in this niche area (125 citations), providing an economically viable technical pathway for low-carbon heating in agricultural greenhouses, and has been widely cited in subsequent energy storage and agricultural energy research.
2. An automated optimization method for calibrating building energy simulation models with measured data: Orientation and a case study (2016) Description: Proposed an automated optimization method for calibrating building energy simulation models against measured data, addressing the discrepancy between simulation results and actual energy consumption by automating the manual calibration process.
Why it wasn’t done before: Building energy models have numerous parameters (thermal properties of materials, equipment efficiency, occupant behavior, etc.), and traditional manual calibration relied heavily on engineer expertise and was extremely time-consuming. Before 2016, automated optimization algorithms were not yet mature in the building simulation domain, and computational costs further constrained large-scale parameter search.
Impact: With 87 citations, it has become an important methodological reference for building energy model calibration, advancing automated calibration practices in digital twin and Building Information Modeling (BIM) domains.
3. Global sensitivity analysis of an urban microclimate system under uncertainty: Design and case study (2017) Description: Systematically introduced Global Sensitivity Analysis (GSA) into urban microclimate simulation, quantifying the uncertainty contribution of each input parameter to urban thermal environment predictions and identifying the most influential factors.
Why it wasn’t done before: Urban microclimate models have extremely high parameter dimensionality (building geometry, materials, vegetation, meteorology, etc.), and local sensitivity analysis cannot capture interaction effects between parameters. Before 2017, the computational overhead of coupling Sobol and other global GSA methods with urban microclimate solvers was prohibitive; it only became feasible after high-performance computing became more accessible.
Impact: Cited 76 times, it established a methodological framework for uncertainty quantification in urban climate models and advanced model credibility assessment in climate-adaptive urban planning.
4. The airborne transmission of infection between flats in high-rise residential buildings: A review (2015) Description: Systematically reviewed the mechanisms of droplet and aerosol transmission between units in high-rise residential buildings, with a focus on cross-floor infection pathways driven by single-sided natural ventilation, and assessed the associated public health risks.
Why it wasn’t done before: Cross-floor aerosol transmission only began attracting scholarly attention after SARS (2003), but sufficient experimental and simulation data did not accumulate to support a systematic review until 2015. High-density high-rise living is a context specific to East Asia, and Western literature had previously provided inadequate coverage.
Impact: Cited 61 times, it was widely referenced during the COVID-19 pandemic and became an important reference for infection risk assessment in high-rise buildings, influencing discussions on ventilation design standards.
5. Planning Low-carbon Distributed Power Systems: Evaluating the Role of Energy Storage (2020) Description: Incorporated energy storage systems (with a dynamic charge/discharge efficiency model) into a generation capacity expansion planning framework, evaluating the techno-economic value of storage in decarbonization pathways for distributed power systems.
Why it wasn’t done before: Early capacity planning models treated energy storage as a static device, ignoring the dynamic nature of charge/discharge efficiency as a function of state. Before 2020, lithium battery costs had not yet fallen dramatically, limiting the commercial relevance of including storage in planning models; accurate modeling only became urgent as storage costs dropped rapidly.
Impact: Cited 46 times, it provides power system planners with a quantitative tool for evaluating storage value, with practical applications in renewable energy integration and grid decarbonization decision support.
Research Directions Building and urban energy system simulation, calibration, and optimization (2015–2021) AI model safety, LLM alignment, and vision-language model security (2024–2026, recent direction) Deep learning system efficiency and hardware acceleration (2019, brief involvement) Renewable energy and energy storage planning (2020, single work) Methodological Evolution From 2015 to 2018, the research methodology was centered on physical simulation: CFD fluid dynamics and building energy simulation engines (EnergyPlus, etc.) paired with optimization algorithms (genetic algorithms, Bayesian optimization) for calibration and sensitivity analysis — a classical paradigm in computational engineering. From 2019 onward, deep learning methods appeared (sparse activation, parallelized training), indicating a fundamental shift in the methodological toolkit. The AI safety work from 2024 to 2025 employs contemporary LLM alignment techniques such as reinforcement learning post-training and chain-of-thought supervision, representing a complete break from the earlier building physics methodology.
This evolution is not an organic progression; it looks more like the product of different researchers with different backgrounds being conflated through database aggregation. If treated as a single individual, the methodological leaps far exceed the pace of a normal academic transition, with no bridging work to connect the phases.
Field Impact This researcher (or group of researchers sharing the same name) has made substantive contributions to building environment engineering: three papers from 2015 to 2017 each exceed 50 citations, establishing a citable methodological foundation in urban microclimate simulation, airborne infection risk in high-rise buildings, and automated building energy calibration. However, only 14 citations in the past five years indicate that no meaningful impact has yet been established in the newly entered AI field, and sustained depth is lacking in every direction. Overall, this is a scholarly profile with moderate influence in a single domain (building energy), highly scattered in recent years with no top-venue output, and not yet possessing cross-domain integrative influence.

content:
Jie Guo — Researcher Analysis Report Analysis Mode: fast | Analysis Time: 2026-03-16T00:52:17
Rating: Active Researcher (33.4/100) Key Metrics Metric Value Affiliation Unknown h-index 11 Total Citations 2,266 Citations (Last 5 Years) 0 Total Papers 15 Top-Venue Papers 0 Publication Span 2000 - 2021 Semantic Scholar 50115592 Research Trajectory A critical issue must be noted upfront: based on the actual content of the paper list, this is not a single researcher’s academic trajectory, but rather the result of incorrectly aggregated data from multiple researchers sharing the same name (郭杰/郭洁). The highly-cited papers from 2000–2005 (with over 2,000 cumulative citations) belong to a public health researcher focused on adolescent substance abuse and social development, published in top public health journals such as the American Journal of Public Health and the Journal of Studies on Alcohol. Then, starting in 2016–2018, materials science papers on thermal conductivity suddenly appear, followed by a shift toward computer vision (scene text detection, object tracking) from 2017 onward. After 2019, the papers span completely unrelated fields including astronomy, 3D Gaussian splatting, quantum physics, dance generation, and recommender systems. Such breadth is impossible for a single researcher.
Consequently, the h-index of 11 and total citation count of 2,266 actually derive from at least two entirely distinct researchers: one public health scholar active from 2000–2005 (responsible for the vast majority of citations), and one or more computer science/materials science researchers active from 2016 onward. The anomalous zero citations over the past five years further corroborates the data aggregation error.
Landmark Works 1. The dynamics of alcohol and marijuana initiation: patterns and predictors of first use in adolescence (2000) Description: Using longitudinal tracking data, this study systematically analyzed the temporal dynamics and predictors of adolescents’ first use of alcohol and marijuana, revealing developmental patterns in the initiation of substance use.
Why it was not done before: Required the accumulation of large-scale longitudinal cohort data and a sufficiently long tracking period spanning adolescence — data of this kind did not mature until the late 1990s. The study also demanded multilevel statistical modeling methods capable of handling repeated measures.
Impact: 444 citations; became a landmark reference in research on adolescent substance use initiation and provided a key predictive framework for subsequent intervention studies.
2. Long-Term Effects of the Seattle Social Development Intervention on School Bonding Trajectories (2001) Description: Evaluated the long-term effects of the Seattle Social Development Project by tracking the developmental trajectories of school bonding, demonstrating the lasting impact of early social intervention.
Why it was not done before: Intervention research requires more than a decade of follow-up to assess long-term outcomes. The high cost of data collection and the difficulty of controlling attrition rates meant that sufficient funding for such large-scale longitudinal intervention studies only became available in the 1990s.
Impact: 416 citations; established an empirical foundation for the effectiveness of early intervention in the field of prevention science, shaping youth health policy for decades.
3. Developmental relationships between adolescent substance use and risky sexual behavior in young adulthood (2002) Description: From a developmental psychology perspective, this study revealed the longitudinal associations between adolescent substance use and risky sexual behavior in early adulthood, proposing a co-developmental pathway hypothesis.
Why it was not done before: Required long-term tracking data spanning adolescence through early adulthood, with simultaneous measurement of multiple risk behaviors. The methodological tools for joint analysis of multiple risk behaviors — such as latent growth curve models — did not fully mature until the early 2000s.
Impact: 298 citations; effectively bridged the fields of substance abuse research and sexual health research, advancing the development of integrated adolescent risk behavior intervention strategies.
4. Family influences on the risk of daily smoking initiation (2005) Description: Systematically analyzed the pathways through which family factors — including parental smoking behavior, family monitoring, and parent-child relationship quality — influence adolescents’ risk of initiating daily smoking.
Why it was not done before: Required matched data collected from both adolescent self-reports and parental reports, while controlling for genetic confounders. The widespread adoption of survival analysis in epidemiology was a key technical prerequisite.
Impact: 289 citations; directly influenced the design of family-based smoking cessation interventions and has been widely cited in tobacco control policy research.
5. Superior thermal conductivity in suspended bilayer hexagonal boron nitride (2016) Description: Used suspended pre-patterned microstructures to measure the in-plane thermal conductivity of exfoliated bilayer hexagonal boron nitride, revealing its exceptional thermal transport properties.
Why it was not done before: Required dry-transfer techniques for preparing high-quality suspended h-BN samples with minimal polymer residue, as well as a precision thermal measurement platform for ultra-thin 2D materials — conditions that only became available around 2015.
Impact: Opened a research direction for hexagonal boron nitride as a thermal management material and established an experimental benchmark for understanding heat transport physics in 2D materials.
Research Areas Adolescent substance abuse and risk behavior development (public health / prevention science, 2000–2005) Thermal conductivity of 2D materials and nano-scale heat transport (materials physics, 2016–2018) Computer vision and 3D content generation (scene text detection, object tracking, 3D Gaussian splatting, dance generation, 2017–2026) Multimodal recommender systems and natural language processing (2022–2026) Quantum information and quantum entanglement measures (2024–2025) Methodological Evolution Because this profile aggregates data from multiple researchers sharing the same name, it is not possible to trace a single coherent methodological evolution. For the public health researcher (2000–2005), the methodology progressed from descriptive epidemiology to longitudinal tracking designs, latent growth curve models, and survival analysis — a trajectory reflecting the maturation of prevention science methodology. For the computer vision researcher cluster (2017–2026), a classic deep learning evolution is observable: CNN feature extraction (2017–2018) → attention mechanisms (2018–2019) → Transformer architectures (2021–2022) → diffusion models and 3D Gaussian splatting (2023–2026), progressing in lockstep with the broader field.
No logical continuity exists between these two methodological threads, further confirming the name-aggregation issue.
Field Impact Evaluated as an independent individual, the “Jie Guo” in public health (2000–2005) demonstrates substantial academic influence: 5 core papers with over 2,000 cumulative citations, multiple publications in top-tier field journals, and a standing as a reliably high-impact researcher in adolescent prevention science. The “Jie Guo” in computer vision and materials science (2016–2026) has a larger volume of papers but limited per-paper citations, reflecting a position of following mainstream technical trends rather than leading them. The overall profile’s h-index of 11 is primarily driven by the public health papers, while zero citations in the past five years reflects the cessation of publishing by the public health researcher. It is recommended to re-evaluate after disambiguating researchers by institutional affiliation or ORCID.

Jun Lu — Researcher Analysis Report Analysis Mode: fast | Analysis Time: 2026-03-16T00:14:36
Rating: Rising Star (57.2/100) Core Metrics Metric Value Affiliation Unknown h-index 27 Total Citations 2,852 Citations (Last 5 Years) 186 Total Papers 207 Top-Venue Papers 13 Publication Span 1989 - 2023 Semantic Scholar 5223928 Research Trajectory Jun Lu’s academic career follows a clear two-phase arc. The first phase (1989–2013) was devoted to numerical electromagnetic field computation, with a particular focus on the Harmonic Balance Finite-Element Method (HB-FEM) and high-frequency planar transformer design, building a robust methodological foundation in nonlinear magnetic field analysis. A pivotal shift occurred around 2013–2015: the global surge in the electric vehicle industry and smart grid policy initiatives gave his accumulated expertise in high-frequency magnetics and wireless charging high industrial relevance, steering his research toward EV wireless charging, microgrid energy management, and energy storage system optimization. The second phase (2014–2023) peaked with a highly cited survey published in 2018 (366 citations), establishing him as a leading voice in the EV wireless charging field. Notably, papers appearing after 2022—covering topics such as superconducting materials, Riemannian geometry, quantum networks, and insect-computer hybrid speakers—indicate severe name-collision data contamination and should be attributed to different researchers, excluded from any assessment of his scholarly output.
Landmark Contributions 1. Analysis of the DC Bias Phenomenon by the Harmonic Balance Finite-Element Method (2011) Description: Systematically applied the Harmonic Balance Finite-Element Method (HB-FEM) to the analysis of the DC bias phenomenon in transformers, establishing a complete mathematical model capable of accurately computing harmonic distributions in nonlinearly saturated magnetic fields.
Why it wasn’t done before: DC bias-induced magnetic saturation is a strongly nonlinear problem; traditional time-domain finite-element methods were computationally prohibitive and prone to convergence failures. Early HB-FEM theory was still immature, lacking the fixed-point iterative algorithms needed to handle hysteresis loop nonlinearity, and available computing power was insufficient to support fully coupled three-dimensional harmonic solutions.
Impact: Provided an authoritative simulation tool for DC bias protection and design in power system transformers. Published in IEEE Transactions on Power Delivery with 51 citations, it became a benchmark reference in this sub-field.
2. Characterizations of High Frequency Planar Transformer With a Novel Comb-Shaped Shield (2011) Description: Proposed a comb-shaped shielding structure for high-frequency planar transformers, systematically characterizing its electromagnetic shielding effectiveness and eddy-current loss behavior, and offering a novel structural approach for on-chip power supply and wireless charging coupler design.
Why it wasn’t done before: At high frequencies, skin effect and proximity effect interact in complex ways that invalidate traditional analytical models. Accurate three-dimensional finite-element simulation combined with experimental validation was required, yet simulation tools of this precision and the microfabrication processes needed were not widely accessible in the early 2000s.
Impact: Cited 45 times, it advanced planar transformers toward higher frequencies and greater power density and established a design paradigm for integrated magnetics in subsequent LLC resonant converter research.
3. Autoregressive with Exogenous Variables and Neural Network Short-Term Load Forecast Models for Residential Low Voltage Distribution Networks (2014) Description: Developed a hybrid ARIMAX and neural network model for short-term load forecasting in residential low-voltage distribution networks, identifying key exogenous variables driving demand and enabling accurate next-day total consumption and peak demand prediction.
Why it wasn’t done before: End-user behavior at the low-voltage distribution level is highly stochastic, and traditional statistical models lacked sufficient generalization ability. Deep learning had not yet matured, and early neural networks suffered from overfitting and training difficulties. It was the widespread rollout of smart meters at this time that made high-resolution historical consumption data available for the first time, making data-driven modeling feasible.
Impact: Cited 80 times, it became an important benchmark in distribution network demand forecasting and directly inspired subsequent research on forecasting modules within microgrid energy management systems.
4. Review of static and dynamic wireless electric vehicle charging system (2018) Description: Provided a comprehensive review of static and dynamic wireless EV charging technologies, covering inductive coupling principles, coil design, power electronics topologies, interoperability standards, and safety considerations, while proposing a technology roadmap.
Why it wasn’t done before: The publication of this review depended on the large body of original research accumulated between 2012 and 2018 as wireless charging technology transitioned from laboratory to industry. Maturation of the Qi standard and the drafting of SAE J2954 made standardization discussions possible, while the author’s decade of prior work on planar transformers, inductive couplers, and EV charging systems gave him the standing to synthesize the field authoritatively.
Impact: Cited 366 times—the most influential work in his entire publication list—this paper established Jun Lu as a representative scholar in EV wireless charging and has been widely cited as a survey entry point by subsequent researchers.
5. Short-term load forecasting for microgrid energy management system using hybrid HHO-FNN model with best-basis stationary wavelet packet transform (2020) Description: Applied best-basis stationary wavelet packet transform for load signal decomposition, combined with a feedforward neural network tuned by the Harris Hawks Optimization (HHO) algorithm, to construct a dedicated short-term load forecasting framework for microgrid energy management.
Why it wasn’t done before: The HHO algorithm was only proposed in 2019 and was a relatively new metaheuristic tool at the time. Hybrid forecasting frameworks combining deep learning with intelligent optimization algorithms required sufficient computational resources and standardized microgrid operational datasets, both of which were inadequate before 2019.
Impact: Cited 105 times, this is the author’s most influential original work at the intersection of forecasting and energy management, establishing the “signal decomposition + metaheuristic optimization + neural network” three-stage forecasting paradigm as a research template in the microgrid domain.
Research Directions Harmonic Balance Finite-Element Method (HB-FEM) and nonlinear electromagnetic field analysis High-frequency planar transformers and integrated magnetic device design Wireless EV charging technology (static and dynamic) Microgrid energy management, energy storage optimization, and control Short-term load forecasting and wind power prediction (data-driven methods) Smart grids and Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) systems Methodological Evolution Jun Lu’s methodology has undergone three paradigm shifts. The first phase (1989–2012) was centered on mathematical analysis: the harmonic balance method transformed nonlinear differential equations into systems of complex algebraic equations, and combined with finite-element discretization, it formed the distinctive HB-FEM computational electromagnetics toolchain. The hallmark of methodological progress in this phase was the upgrade from simple harmonic truncation to fixed-point iteration and neural-network-assisted hysteresis modeling. The second phase (2013–2018) shifted toward system-level modeling and control: the research scope expanded from magnetic devices to power electronics converter topologies and microgrid hierarchical control architectures, and the methodology extended from numerical simulation (FEM) to experimental platform validation and hardware-in-the-loop testing, with MEMS RF switches and wireless charging coupler design also incorporating microfabrication perspectives. The third phase (2019–2023) fully embraced the data-driven paradigm: the combination of wavelet packet decomposition, metaheuristic optimization (HHO, GWO, SSA), and deep neural networks (LSTM) reflects the broader migration of energy systems research toward machine learning methods. The author adeptly transferred his signal processing expertise to time-series forecasting problems, maintaining methodological coherence throughout.
Field Impact Jun Lu’s core contributions to the field operate on two levels. At the foundational methods level, his HB-FEM series (1989–2017) provided mature computational tools for analyzing DC bias in power transformers and high-frequency nonlinear magnetic fields; the resulting work, published in top journals including IEEE Transactions on Magnetics and IEEE Transactions on Power Delivery, retains enduring reference value in the academic community. At the applied level, his 2018 EV wireless charging review (366 citations), by virtue of its comprehensiveness and timeliness, became essential reading for researchers entering the field, effectively shaping the research agenda. Overall, Jun Lu represents a capable, application-oriented engineering scholar: with an h-index of 27 and approximately 2,852 total citations, he holds a solid academic influence at the intersection of power electronics and smart grids. However, with only 13 top-venue papers and relatively modest recent citations (186 over the last five years), his peak period has passed and his current influence rests primarily on the long-tail citations of his historical output.

L. Teng — Researcher Analysis Report Analysis mode: fast | Analysis time: 2026-03-16T00:17:54
Rating: Active Researcher (45.5/100) Basic Metrics Metric Value Affiliation Unknown h-index 21 Total Citations 1,607 Citations (last 5 years) 63 Total Publications 55 Top-venue Publications 0 Publication Years 2008 - 2025 Semantic Scholar 49286689 Research Trajectory L. Teng’s academic trajectory is highly fragmented, with publications spanning completely unrelated fields — glioma treatment, cardiac stem cells, NADPH oxidase vascular biology, marine model organism immunology, and GaAs quantum well spin relaxation — strongly suggesting that this name aggregates multiple distinct researchers (a name disambiguation problem). Focusing on the primary research thread, one can identify a core body of work centered on glioma diagnosis and treatment: the field was entered in 2011 with the landmark review Aberrant Signaling Pathways in Glioma (219 citations), followed by a series of high-impact mechanistic studies on 5-ALA fluorescence-guided surgery and photodynamic therapy between 2011 and 2013, which constitute the main source of academic influence.
The pivotal turning point in this career trajectory was the concentrated burst of output during 2011–2013 — by elucidating the regulatory mechanisms of ferrochelatase silencing and ABCB6 overexpression on protoporphyrin IX accumulation, this work provided molecular targets for improving clinical 5-ALA fluorescence surgery quality, with three representative papers accumulating over 450 citations combined. However, only 63 citations in the past five years (out of 1,607 lifetime) indicate that research impact has declined markedly since 2018, with new output increasingly scattered across topics (C-phycocyanin, zebrafish, SGLT-2) without establishing a sustained core research program.
Breakthrough Works 1. Aberrant Signaling Pathways in Glioma (2011) Description: A systematic review of aberrant activation mechanisms of key signaling pathways in glioblastoma (GBM) — including EGFR, PDGFR, PTEN/PI3K/Akt, Rb/CDK4, and p53 — providing a theoretical framework for targeted therapeutic strategies.
Why this wasn’t done before: Accumulation of the key insights took time: only after the 2007 TCGA glioma genomic atlas data release could researchers systematically integrate the full picture of multi-pathway aberrations; prior to this, individual pathway studies were relatively isolated, lacking a cross-pathway integrative perspective.
Impact: Became a highly cited review in the glioma signaling pathway field (219 citations), providing a standard reference framework for subsequent targeted drug development and combination therapy strategies.
2. Silencing of ferrochelatase enhances 5-aminolevulinic acid-based fluorescence and photodynamic therapy efficacy (2011) Description: Discovered that silencing ferrochelatase (FECH) blocks the metabolic conversion of PpIX to heme, thereby significantly enhancing both fluorescence intensity and photodynamic therapy efficacy of 5-ALA in glioma cells.
Why this wasn’t done before: 5-ALA had been used clinically for fluorescence-guided surgery for years, but insufficient PpIX accumulation limited fluorescence quality. The breakthrough lay in identifying FECH as the key metabolic bottleneck — which required a detailed enzymatic map of the 5-ALA metabolic pathway and mature gene silencing technology (siRNA), both of which became simultaneously available around 2010.
Impact: Cited 158 times, pioneering the research direction of improving 5-ALA fluorescence quality through metabolic intervention and providing an actionable target for optimizing clinical fluorescence-guided surgery.
3. Increased Expression of ABCB6 Enhances Protoporphyrin IX Accumulation and Photodynamic Effect in Human Glioma (2013) Description: Revealed that overexpression of the ATP-binding cassette transporter ABCB6 promotes PpIX accumulation in mitochondria, enhancing photodynamic therapy sensitivity in glioma through a complementary mechanism.
Why this wasn’t done before: The functional role of ABCB6 in porphyrin transport was only preliminarily established in 2009; connecting it to glioma PDT sensitization required cross-disciplinary integration (mitochondrial transport biology + tumor photodynamic therapy), a connection that had not previously been made.
Impact: Cited 98 times, together with the ferrochelatase study establishing a dual-target strategy for regulating PpIX metabolism, driving a wave of mechanistic research in glioma photodynamic therapy.
4. The Heme Oxygenase 1 Inducer (CoPP) Protects Human Cardiac Stem Cells against Apoptosis through Activation of the Extracellular Signal-regulated Kinase (ERK)/NRF2 Signaling Pathway and Cytokine Release (2012) Description: First demonstrated that the HO-1 inducer CoPP can enhance post-transplant survival of human cardiac stem cells (hCSCs) through the ERK/NRF2 pathway and cytokine secretion, addressing the core obstacle of massive transplanted cell death in stem cell therapy.
Why this wasn’t done before: The clinical translational potential of c-kit+ cardiac stem cells was only demonstrated in 2003, and their extremely low post-transplant survival rate (>90% dying within 7 days) had become a bottleneck. Applying the cytoprotective mechanisms of the HO-1/CO system to cardiac stem cell preconditioning required systematic integration of knowledge from two independent fields (HO-1 cardiovascular protection and stem cell therapy).
Impact: Cited 97 times, establishing the paradigm of a “preconditioning + stem cell transplantation” combined strategy and advancing the field of cell survival research in cardiac regenerative medicine.
5. The synergistic antitumor effects of all-trans retinoic acid and C-phycocyanin on the lung cancer A549 cells in vitro and in vivo (2015) Description: Demonstrated in both in vitro and in vivo models that the combination of all-trans retinoic acid (ATRA) and C-phycocyanin exerts synergistic antitumor effects against lung cancer A549 cells, providing experimental evidence for combining natural products with differentiation-inducing therapy.
Why this wasn’t done before: The antitumor mechanisms of C-phycocyanin were only gradually elucidated in the early 2010s; combining it with the established ATRA differentiation therapy strategy required cross-mechanistic pathway research, and sufficient data accumulation to support systematic combination studies was only available around 2015.
Impact: Cited 86 times, one of the landmark studies in the field of C-phycocyanin antitumor applications, advancing the exploration of this natural pigment in tumor pharmacology.
Research Directions Glioma diagnosis and treatment (5-ALA fluorescence-guided surgery mechanism optimization, photodynamic therapy sensitization, signaling pathway-targeted therapy, glioma stem cells) Cardiac stem cells and myocardial protection (HO-1/NO preconditioning strategies, ERK/NRF2 survival pathways) NADPH oxidase and endothelial cell oxidative stress (p47phox phosphorylation regulatory mechanisms) Marine/zebrafish model organism immunity and developmental biology (defensins, Ly6 family, MOV10, CD248) Methodological Evolution Early work (2008–2010) relied primarily on classical molecular biology techniques: western blot, gene knockout mouse models, and protein phosphorylation analysis, mainly investigating the regulatory mechanisms of NADPH oxidase. The 2011–2014 period represented methodological maturity, incorporating RNAi gene silencing, fluorescence microscopy, and in vitro/in vivo photodynamic therapy models; experimental design shifted from single-molecule mechanisms toward full-chain validation of “target → intervention → functional phenotype,” driving the production of the highest-impact papers. Post-2015, the methodology became notably fragmented: while in vitro/in vivo tumor models continued, zebrafish developmental models, phylogenetic analysis, and reproductive toxicology assays were introduced — entirely different methodological frameworks — reflecting a lack of sustained research focus. In recent years (2024–2025), the work has shifted toward clinical observational research, with published cardiology clinical reviews bearing almost no methodological continuity with the earlier basic research.
Field Impact In the specialized subfield of glioma 5-ALA metabolic regulation, L. Teng (as the glioma research entity) made substantive contributions: through the discovery of two complementary mechanistic targets — ferrochelatase and ABCB6 — the work systematically elucidated the metabolic network governing PpIX accumulation, and has been frequently cited by subsequent research in the field; the h-index of 21 is primarily supported by this period. However, because the main research thread lost focus after 2017, a sustained research program around 5-ALA surgery optimization or glioma-targeted therapy was never established, missing the window to translate early mechanistic discoveries into clinical applications. Overall assessment: an effective contributor to a specific subfield, rather than a leading figure in the broader domain; the sharp decline in impact over the past five years (only 63 citations) indicates that academic influence has entered a plateau.
