content:
Linghao Song — Researcher Analysis Report Analysis Mode: fast | Analysis Time: 2026-03-16T00:46:11
Rating: Early-Stage Researcher (22.0/100) Basic Metrics Metric Value Affiliation Unknown h-index 2 Total Citations 33 Citations (Last 5 Years) 22 Total Papers 3 Top-Venue Papers 0 Publication Years 2020 - 2023 Semantic Scholar 2150597993 Research Trajectory Linghao Song is a computer architecture researcher whose trajectory consistently centers on in/near-memory computing. In 2017, his ReRAM-based graph processing accelerator GraphR won a Best Paper Award, establishing his early reputation in non-volatile memory computing. His most prolific period (2020–2022) saw him systematically build a complete research ecosystem around sparse linear algebra acceleration (SpMV, SpMM, conjugate gradient solvers) and FPGA high-level synthesis frameworks (TAPA). In 2023, NDSEARCH appeared at ISCA — a top computer architecture venue — adapting near-data processing to vector databases and LLM retrieval-augmented generation, completing a pivotal transition from traditional HPC to AI systems.
A defining characteristic of his trajectory is a dual-track “tooling + applications” approach: the TAPA/RapidStream-IR series provides FPGA programming infrastructure, while the Serpens/Sextans/NDSEARCH series represents high-impact applications built on that infrastructure. This in-house toolchain strategy gives his research strong reproducibility and community reach. Note that two cosmology/neutrino papers from 2024 (related to the Seesaw mechanism) are entirely disconnected from the rest of his work and most likely belong to a different researcher with the same name; they are excluded from this analysis.
Breakthrough Works 1. GraphR: Accelerating Graph Processing Using ReRAM (2017) Description: Proposed the first ReRAM-based graph processing accelerator, mapping graph adjacency matrices directly onto ReRAM crossbar arrays and using analog circuits to perform massively parallel matrix-vector multiplication — bypassing the memory bandwidth bottleneck of traditional von Neumann architectures and achieving extremely energy-efficient graph computation.
Why It Wasn’t Done Before: Before 2015, ReRAM device fabrication was not mature enough — device-to-device variability was too high for precise conductance control. More importantly, the key insight was missing: prior researchers treated ReRAM purely as a digital storage replacement. GraphR was the first to systematically argue that graph computation is highly tolerant of numerical imprecision and that sparse matrices are naturally suited to analog-domain parallelism, making analog errors acceptable.
Impact: Pioneered the “ReRAM as a compute substrate” research direction. The Best Paper Award cemented it as a landmark work in near-memory computing, directly spawning a large body of follow-on ReRAM accelerator research. Its methodology — mapping sparse matrices onto analog arrays — has been widely cited and extended.
2. Refloat: Low-Cost Floating-Point Processing in ReRAM for Accelerating Iterative Linear Solvers (2020) Description: Tackled the central engineering challenge in ReRAM analog computing: floating-point support. The work proposes decomposing floating-point operations into forms that can be executed at low cost in ReRAM, applied to iterative linear solvers (a core operator in scientific computing), and was published at SC, the top supercomputing venue.
Why It Wasn’t Done Before: Floating-point operations demand high precision (IEEE 754 single-precision requires a 23-bit mantissa), yet ReRAM analog computation is inherently noisy. The prevailing assumption was that “ReRAM is only suitable for low-precision integer/fixed-point arithmetic.” The missing insight was that iterative solvers in scientific computing have self-convergent properties, allowing intermediate steps to use mixed precision. Refloat found a controllable trade-off between precision error and convergence speed — a conceptual breakthrough, not merely an engineering refinement.
Impact: Extended the applicability of ReRAM acceleration from graph and neural network workloads to scientific computing, opening up a new HPC+ReRAM research space. Its 11 citations represent solid performance for an SC paper.
3. TAPA: A Scalable Task-Parallel Dataflow Programming Framework for Modern FPGAs with Co-Optimization of HLS and Physical Design (2022) Description: Proposed TAPA, an end-to-end compilation framework that allows users to describe computation as task-parallel dataflow programs in C++, automatically compiling them into high-frequency FPGA accelerators. The key innovation is the co-optimization of high-level synthesis (HLS) and physical floorplanning, addressing the long-standing problem of frequency collapse in large-scale FPGA designs.
Why It Wasn’t Done Before: Mainstream tools such as Xilinx Vitis HLS treated HLS and physical implementation as separate stages, causing the logic generated by HLS to suffer severe frequency degradation after place-and-route. The missing insight was that task-parallel granularity naturally aligns with the physical partitioning of FPGA SLRs (Super Logic Regions) — if physical constraints are made visible at the HLS stage, high productivity and high performance can be achieved simultaneously. This requires deep understanding of both FPGA architecture and HLS internals, and exemplifies cross-layer abstraction capability.
Impact: Became an important infrastructure tool for academic FPGA accelerator research. TAPA-CS subsequently extended its reach to distributed multi-FPGA scenarios, lowering the barrier to developing high-performance FPGA accelerators.
4. NDSEARCH: Accelerating Graph-Traversal-Based Approximate Nearest Neighbor Search through Near Data Processing (2023) Description: Proposed a near-data processing acceleration solution for graph-traversal-based approximate nearest neighbor search (ANNS, e.g., HNSW/NSG algorithms) in vector databases, published at ISCA (a top computer architecture venue), directly targeting the retrieval engine underpinning LLM retrieval-augmented generation (RAG).
Why It Wasn’t Done Before: Before 2022, ANNS graph-traversal algorithms were treated primarily as software/algorithmic problems. The explosive growth of LLM+RAG in 2022–2023 was what made ANNS a critical datacenter workload, creating strong motivation for hardware acceleration. On the architecture side, there was previously insufficient empirical evidence to demonstrate that ANNS is memory-bandwidth-bound rather than compute-bound; NDSEARCH provided the key evidence through profiling, making the near-data processing approach compelling.
Impact: With 22 citations, this is his most-cited work to date, arriving at precisely the right moment in the LLM infrastructure wave. It redirected the architecture community’s attention toward vector database workloads within the near-memory computing space, spurring a substantial body of follow-on ANNS accelerator research.
Research Areas Near/in-memory computing and hardware accelerator design (ReRAM, HBM, NDP) Sparse linear algebra and graph computation acceleration (SpMV, SpMM, graph traversal) FPGA high-level synthesis and programming frameworks (TAPA, RapidStream) System acceleration for AI/LLM infrastructure (ANNS, vector databases, RAG) Methodological Evolution In the early phase (2017–2019), the dominant paradigm was analog computing — exploiting ReRAM’s physical properties for matrix operations, with the methodological core being a precision-versus-efficiency trade-off analysis. From 2020–2021, the focus shifted to digital sparse accelerator design; the methodology evolved into “dataflow analysis + memory access pattern modeling,” addressing load imbalance and irregular memory access in sparse computation. The release of the Pyxis dataset also reflects a growing emphasis on rigorous evaluation methodology. In 2022, a “cross-layer co-optimization” methodology was introduced: TAPA simultaneously spans the programming model, HLS compiler, and physical design layers, signaling an expansion of research scope from individual accelerators to complete toolchains. By 2023, the methodology had further evolved toward “workload-driven architecture design”: NDSEARCH begins with performance bottleneck analysis of real LLM application scenarios and works backward to hardware architecture decisions, reflecting a mature system research paradigm anchored in application requirements.
Overall, the methodological evolution follows the path: analog near-memory computing → digital sparse acceleration → compiler–hardware co-design → AI-application-driven architecture. The persistent thread throughout is a sustained focus on the memory bandwidth bottleneck; the means of addressing it have simply evolved from analog computation to HBM utilization to near-data processing.
Field Impact Linghao Song’s contributions to computer architecture span two dimensions. The first is directional impact: GraphR (2017 Best Paper) and NDSEARCH (ISCA 2023) each sent a guiding research signal at a pivotal moment — the former inspiring a generation of ReRAM accelerator work, the latter arriving at the peak of LLM infrastructure interest and triggering broad follow-up. The second is infrastructure impact: the TAPA framework, used as an open-source tool, has lowered the barrier to entry for FPGA accelerator research and produced a multiplier effect. Looking at citation distribution, NDSEARCH’s 22 citations accumulated within just one to two years of its 2023 publication, indicating strong recent influence at the intersection of AI systems and computer architecture — closely aligned with current LLM infrastructure research trends.

Nathan Inkawhich — Researcher Analysis Report Analysis Mode: fast | Analysis Time: 2026-03-16T00:44:17
Rating: Rising Star (55.8/100) Basic Metrics Metric Value Affiliation Unknown h-index 14 Total Citations 1,026 Citations (Last 5 Years) 442 Total Papers 39 Top-Venue Papers 12 Publication Years 2017 - 2025 Semantic Scholar 52121635 Research Trajectory Nathan Inkawhich is a researcher working at the intersection of adversarial machine learning and SAR (Synthetic Aperture Radar) remote sensing recognition. Since 2017, he has developed in parallel along two tracks: defense/remote sensing applications and deep learning security. His academic breakthrough came in 2019-2020, with consecutive publications at CVPR, NeurIPS (two papers), and ICLR that established his reputation in transferable black-box adversarial attacks. These four top-venue papers have collectively accumulated over 500 citations, representing the pinnacle of his academic career.
From 2021 onward, he underwent a systematic shift in research focus: moving from “how to craft stronger attacks” to “how to build more reliable recognition systems,” concentrating on open-world SAR-ATR (Automatic Target Recognition), out-of-distribution (OOD) detection, and multi-modal remote sensing image understanding, forming a central theme of “safe and reliable AI recognition systems.” Since 2023, he has begun systematically evaluating the applicability and vulnerabilities of foundation models (CLIP, DINOv2, etc.) in adversarial settings and SAR recognition, expanding his research scope from attack-defense mechanism studies to practical evaluation of foundation models.
Breakthrough Works 1. Feature Space Perturbations Yield More Transferable Adversarial Examples (2019) Description: Proposes a black-box transferable adversarial attack method based on deep feature space perturbations. Unlike the prevailing output-layer gradient attacks, this work perturbs intermediate feature layers, significantly improving the targeted adversarial transfer success rate in black-box settings while also providing interpretability analysis of the model’s internal representations.
Why It Wasn’t Done Before: Prior research generally assumed adversarial perturbations should target the output layer (softmax/logit layer) to directly manipulate classification results, with limited systematic understanding of the semantic structure of intermediate feature layers. The key missing insight was that the class-separability structure of feature layers — rather than the decision boundary at the output layer — is the fundamental source of transferability. This work was the first to systematically link feature distribution modeling with transferability mechanisms.
Impact: Cited 199 times, it has become a key baseline reference for subsequent transferable adversarial attack research, driving a paradigm shift in the field from “output-layer attacks” to “feature-layer attacks,” and inspiring a series of subsequent attack-defense works based on intermediate layer representations.
2. Transferable Perturbations of Deep Feature Distributions (2020) Description: By modeling the class-level distributions (mean and covariance) of deep features, this work designs an adversarial attack method that operates entirely without output-layer information, achieving state-of-the-art targeted black-box transfer attack performance at the time.
Why It Wasn’t Done Before: Prior feature-layer attack methods typically targeted individual feature activation points rather than statistically modeling the overall feature distribution. Accurately estimating class-conditional feature distributions requires a large number of same-class samples and corresponding distribution modeling techniques — a perspective that had not yet been incorporated into adversarial attack design. Compute was not the bottleneck; the key was introducing the statistical lens of “feature distributions.”
Impact: Cited 98 times (published at ICLR), this work introduced statistical distribution modeling into adversarial attack design, providing a methodological foundation for subsequent generative adversarial examples and feature-matching-based attack methods.
3. Perturbing Across the Feature Hierarchy to Improve Standard and Strict Blackbox Attack Transferability (2020) Description: Proposes the Perturbing Across the Feature Hierarchy (PAH) method, which simultaneously applies perturbations across multiple feature layers with weighted aggregation. This improves standard black-box transferability while maintaining effectiveness under stricter black-box constraints (no label space overlap between source and target models).
Why It Wasn’t Done Before: Prior work typically selected a single feature layer for attack, lacking the structural insight that features at different layers contain complementary adversarial information. Multi-layer joint optimization poses engineering challenges in objective design and gradient balancing; this work resolved the inter-layer weight allocation problem through systematic ablation experiments.
Impact: Cited 100 times (NeurIPS 2020), this work complements the ICLR paper from the same year, together forming Inkawhich’s complete theoretical framework for feature-layer adversarial attacks.
4. DVERGE: Diversifying Vulnerabilities for Enhanced Robust Generation of Ensembles (2020) Description: Discovers that CNN ensemble models exhibit adversarial vulnerability overlap — different models fail in the same input directions — and proposes the DVERGE method, which actively diversifies the adversarial vulnerabilities of each sub-model to enhance the overall robustness of the ensemble, making attacks targeted at a single sub-model difficult to transfer to the entire ensemble.
Why It Wasn’t Done Before: Ensemble learning had long been regarded as a natural solution for adversarial robustness, but the core problem of “vulnerability overlap” had not been systematically quantified or studied. A theoretical understanding of transferable adversarial attacks — precisely the subject of Inkawhich’s own prior work — was first needed before this mechanism could be leveraged in reverse to design diversified training objectives.
Impact: Cited 128 times (NeurIPS 2020), this work translates theoretical understanding of adversarial transferability into defense design principles, opening up a research direction for ensemble robustness based on “vulnerability diversification.”
5. Bridging a Gap in SAR-ATR: Training on Fully Synthetic and Testing on Measured Data (2021) Description: Systematically studies the domain adaptation problem (Sim-to-Real gap) of training on fully synthetic SAR data and testing on real measured data, proposing a transfer learning framework for SAR-ATR. It demonstrates that synthetic data can effectively support model training in defense scenarios where labeled real-world data is extremely scarce.
Why It Wasn’t Done Before: The computational cost of SAR synthetic data generation (electromagnetic scattering simulation) dropped significantly in the late 2010s, and deep domain adaptation methods matured around the same time. Only the combination of these two developments made the Sim-to-Real direction practically feasible for SAR-ATR. Previously, both insufficient data (extremely few labeled real SAR samples) and inadequate simulation fidelity constituted dual bottlenecks.
Impact: Cited 97 times, this work provides a practical, low-data-cost path for training SAR-ATR models in the defense/remote sensing community, serving as a widely cited benchmark in this direction.
Research Areas Transferable Black-box Adversarial Attacks (Feature-level Distribution Modeling) SAR Automatic Target Recognition (SAR-ATR) and Open-World Robustness Out-of-Distribution Detection (OOD Detection) and Fine-grained Recognition Multi-modal Remote Sensing Image Understanding (SAR+EO Fusion) Foundation Model Applicability Assessment in Adversarial Settings and SAR Domains Methodological Evolution Inkawhich’s methodological evolution went through three distinct phases. Phase 1 (2018-2020): centered on offense, starting from pixel-space adversarial perturbations and progressively moving deeper into feature layers — first with single-layer feature mean perturbations (CVPR 2019), then class-level feature distribution modeling (ICLR 2020), and finally extending to cross-layer hierarchical joint perturbations (NeurIPS 2020), forming a complete theoretical framework for feature-space adversarial attacks. DVERGE then inverted the attack mechanism into a defense tool, completing the attack-defense loop.
Phase 2 (2021-2023): the methodology shifted from “crafting adversarial examples” to “building reliable recognition systems.” In the SAR-ATR direction, this involved introducing Sim-to-Real transfer learning and few-shot learning; in the OOD detection direction, data augmentation strategies such as Mixup Outlier Exposure were introduced, with exploration of fine-grained and hierarchical OOD inference. Method design placed greater emphasis on practical scenario constraints (data scarcity, open-world settings, and deployment environment variation). Phase 3 (2023-present): shifted to a systematic evaluation perspective, conducting comprehensive benchmarking of the adversarial robustness and SAR applicability of foundation models such as CLIP and DINOv2, with the methodology evolving from “proposing new methods” to “establishing evaluation frameworks and understanding boundaries.”
Field Impact Inkawhich’s core contributions to the field are concentrated at two intersections: first, introducing statistical distribution modeling of feature distributions into transferable adversarial attack design, driving deeper understanding of the mechanism behind “why adversarial examples can transfer across models” — his four top-venue papers from 2019-2020 collectively exceed 520 citations, making them key reference works in this sub-field; second, making systematic contributions to the practical application of SAR intelligent recognition, covering core challenges including Sim-to-Real transfer, few-shot learning, open-world OOD detection, and multi-modal fusion, with strong practical guidance value for the defense remote sensing AI community. Overall, he is a solid mid-career researcher with a distinctive domain identity: he has established a unique research presence at the intersection of adversarial ML and remote sensing recognition, but his influence remains primarily concentrated in specific sub-fields and has not yet formed the broad, foundational impact that defines an entire research direction.

Wu Lin — Researcher Profile Analysis Analysis Mode: fast | Analysis Date: 2026-03-16T00:19:55
Rating: Rising Star (52.2/100) Core Metrics Metric Value Affiliation Unknown h-index 26 Total Citations 2,130 Citations (Last 5 Years) 125 Total Publications 56 Top-Venue Papers 0 Publication Span 2001 - 2025 Semantic Scholar 8531953 Research Trajectory An important caveat: this publication list suffers from severe name-disambiguation issues. “Wu Lin” is a common Chinese name, and the database has aggregated work from at least 5 distinct researchers across different fields: ① a plant-soil ecologist (replant disease/rhizosphere microbiome, 2001–2025, forming the central thread); ② a computer vision researcher (person re-identification, 2018+); ③ a quantum plasmonics physicist (2018+); ④ a machine learning optimization researcher (natural gradient, KFAC, 2021+); ⑤ a radio astronomer (2019+). The following analysis focuses on the primary researcher with a coherent academic narrative, concentrated citation record, and thematically consistent body of work — Wu Lin working in plant rhizosphere microecology.
This researcher’s core academic contribution lies in systematically elucidating the rhizosphere microecological mechanisms underlying replant disease in Chinese medicinal herbs. Their career unfolds across three clearly defined phases: Phase I (2001–2011) began from traditional agronomy, pioneering the application of metaproteomics to rhizosphere soil research and establishing Rehmannia glutinosa as a model system and technical platform for studying replant disease. Phase II (2013–2018) focused on mechanistic dissection — through chemical analysis of root exudates (HPLC), high-throughput sequencing (pyrosequencing, Illumina), and metagenomics, this work constructed a causal chain of “phenolic acid accumulation → pathogen proliferation → rhizosphere microbial imbalance → yield decline under monoculture.” Phase III (2018–2022) shifted toward intervention strategies, exploring the remediation potential of bio-organic fertilizers and intercropping systems against replant disease, completing a transition from mechanistic inquiry to application-oriented research.
Landmark Works 1. Characterization of metaproteomics in crop rhizospheric soil (2011) Description: The first systematic application of metaproteomics to crop rhizosphere soil research, directly identifying functional microbial proteins in rhizosphere soil and revealing the actual metabolic activity of rhizosphere microbial communities — rather than relying solely on 16S rRNA gene data to infer “potential presence.”
Why it wasn’t done before: Technical bottlenecks: the complex matrix of rhizosphere soil and severe humic acid interference made protein extraction and mass spectrometry identification immature before 2011; additionally, insufficient coverage of soil microbial protein sequences in databases limited identification depth. The maturation of high-throughput mass spectrometry and bioinformatics pipelines made this breakthrough possible.
Impact: Pioneered the field of agricultural soil metaproteomics in China; cited 130 times. It provided the core methodological framework for subsequent replant disease mechanism research and drove a shift in the field from community structure description toward functional analysis.
2. Plant-microbe rhizosphere interactions mediated by Rehmannia glutinosa root exudates under consecutive monoculture (2015) Description: Through a four-year field experiment combined with laboratory validation, this work demonstrated that phenolic acids secreted by Rehmannia glutinosa roots exert a cumulative effect under sterile conditions, directly causing structural imbalance in the rhizosphere microbial community. Key pathogenic secondary metabolites were identified, and a ternary interaction model of “root exudates–microbiome–plant health” was established.
Why it wasn’t done before: Missing key insight: earlier research attributed replant disease to soil nutrient depletion or individual pathogens, lacking a systemic understanding of plant–microbe chemical interactions. The long-term experimental accumulation required by a four-year field trial, combined with the wider availability of HPLC-MS/MS technology, were prerequisite conditions.
Impact: Cited 135 times; became a landmark paper in replant disease research. It provided the first complete chemical ecological evidence chain for Rehmannia glutinosa replant disease and propelled the sustainable cultivation of Chinese medicinal herbs into an independent research direction.
3. The role of organic acids on microbial deterioration in the Radix pseudostellariae rhizosphere under continuous monoculture regimes (2017) Description: Using Radix pseudostellariae (Pseudostellaria heterophylla) as the study system, a three-year field trial combined with transcriptome sequencing clarified at the molecular level how organic acids (oxalic acid, succinic acid, etc.) selectively promote the proliferation of pathogens (Talaromyces helicus, Kosakonia sacchari), revealing the targeted microbial regulatory mechanisms of phenolic compounds.
Why it wasn’t done before: The cost of transcriptome sequencing dropped sharply after 2015, making gene expression analysis of microbial responses to organic acids feasible. Moreover, pathogen identification results from prior work (2016, Frontiers in Microbiology) provided well-defined research targets for this study.
Impact: Cited 94 times; advanced replant disease mechanism research to the level of gene regulation, providing a theoretical basis for targeted biocontrol strategies such as screening antagonistic microbes capable of degrading specific organic acids.
4. Belowground Interactions Impact the Soil Bacterial Community, Soil Fertility, and Crop Yield in Maize/Peanut Intercropping Systems (2018) Description: Using a physical barrier experimental design (non-separated / nylon mesh semi-separation / plastic film complete separation), this study quantified the independent contributions of direct root contact, root exudate exchange, and competitive nutrient uptake to the rhizosphere microbiome in maize/peanut intercropping systems — for the first time disentangling the distinct physical pathways of rhizosphere interaction.
Why it wasn’t done before: Experimental design innovation: prior intercropping research could not distinguish between different physical mechanisms of root interaction. The three-treatment barrier design was a key methodological breakthrough, and the falling cost of 16S amplicon sequencing made large-scale sample analysis feasible.
Impact: Cited 106 times; provided mechanistic grounding for intercropping system optimization and advanced the concept of “engineered rhizosphere microecology,” with direct practical value for sustainable agriculture.
5. Antagonistic Activity of Trichoderma spp. Against Fusarium oxysporum in Rhizosphere of Radix pseudostellariae Triggers the Expression of Host Defense Genes and Improves Its Growth Under Long-Term Monoculture System (2021) Description: Demonstrated that Trichoderma spp. not only directly antagonizes the pathogenic Fusarium oxysporum, but also activates the expression of Radix pseudostellariae’s own defense genes (PR gene family), achieving an “indirect immune priming” effect — completing the transition from passive mechanistic dissection to active intervention validation.
Why it wasn’t done before: Required interdisciplinary capacity integrating macroscale agronomic experiments with molecular plant pathology analysis, and depended on prior identification of the causal pathogen (Fusarium oxysporum, established in the 2015 and 2017 studies) before an effective biocontrol experiment could be designed.
Impact: Cited 53 times; provided direct applied evidence for biocontrol strategies against replant disease, marking the researcher’s entry into a new phase of translating mechanistic findings into green agricultural solutions.
Research Directions Rhizosphere chemical ecology mechanisms of replant disease in Chinese medicinal herbs (phenolic/organic acid–microbe–plant interactions) Rhizosphere microbiome diversity and functional analysis (metaproteomics, metagenomics, high-throughput sequencing) Remediation strategies for rhizosphere microecology via intercropping systems and bio-organic fertilizers Relationships between soil microbial communities and vegetation type / forest succession Methodological Evolution This researcher’s methodological evolution displays a clearly technology-driven character. Early work (2001–2011) relied on traditional microbial culture techniques and two-dimensional electrophoresis; the 2011 introduction of metaproteomics was a first major technological leap, marking a paradigm shift from “culture-dependent” to “culture-independent” approaches. From 2013 to 2016, as pyrosequencing became widely available, the researcher extensively adopted 16S rRNA amplicon sequencing combined with CLPP (community-level physiological profiling) to characterize community structure, while introducing HPLC for quantitative analysis of root exudates — enabling association between “microbial community shifts” and “chemical driving factors.” From 2017 onward, the introduction of transcriptome sequencing (RNA-seq) deepened the research to the level of gene regulation, revealing the molecular mechanisms by which microbes respond to chemical signals. From 2018 to 2022, the methodology became increasingly integrative: metagenomics (functional potential prediction) was combined with field agronomic indicators (yield, enzyme activity, soil nutrients), forming a dual-track validation system linking mechanism with outcome. Throughout the entire trajectory, the researcher consistently maintained a “field validation + laboratory mechanistic analysis” dual-track approach, avoiding the disconnection between pure omics research and real-world agricultural practice.
Field Impact This Wu Lin has made foundational contributions to the rhizosphere ecology of replant disease in Chinese medicinal herbs. Their core impact operates at two levels: methodologically, they pioneered the systematic introduction of modern omics technologies — metaproteomics, metagenomics — into Chinese medicinal herb rhizosphere research, providing a technical model for the entire field; mechanistically, the causal chain of “root exudates (phenolic/organic acids) → rhizosphere microbial dysbiosis → yield decline under monoculture” has now become field consensus, extensively cited and extended by subsequent work. Given the bibliometric profile — h-index of 26, 2,130 total citations, but only 125 in the past five years — the peak of this researcher’s academic influence occurred during 2011–2018, with core contributions now fully absorbed by the field. Their research directly addresses the sustainable cultivation of traditional Chinese medicinal herbs, carrying clear industrial application value beyond its academic merit, and has driven the uptake of bio-organic fertilizers and intercropping systems in medicinal herb production. Note: the Semantic Scholar database has merged publications from multiple namesake researchers (computer vision, quantum physics, radio astronomy, etc.) into this profile; the analysis above has excluded papers clearly not belonging to the same researcher.

content:
Xiaoxiao Liu — Researcher Analysis Report Analysis Mode: fast | Analysis Time: 2026-03-16T00:47:49
Rating: Active Researcher (40.2/100) Basic Metrics Metric Value Affiliation Unknown h-index 12 Total Citations 451 Citations (Last 5 Years) 174 Total Papers 23 Top Conference Papers 0 Publication Years 2005 - 2023 Semantic Scholar 46522031 Research Trajectory It should first be noted that this paper list exhibits a clear name disambiguation issue. The papers span completely unrelated fields including cancer biology, GPU architecture, plant immunity, hyperspectral image processing, genomics, and LLM prompt engineering, clearly aggregated from multiple researchers sharing the same name. Based on publication year, citation count, and thematic coherence, at least 3 distinct Xiaoxiao Liu researchers can be identified: (1) a molecular oncology researcher (accounting for the vast majority of the 451 citations, including the highest-cited miR-1271 pancreatic cancer paper with 62 citations and publications in Nature Communications/JEM/PNAS); (2) a computer architecture/image processing researcher (GPU memory design, hyperspectral denoising); (3) an LLM/AI security researcher (prompt engineering, safety evaluation in 2023–2025). The following analysis focuses primarily on the most representative and best-documented molecular oncology researcher, whose research trajectory shows a progressive deepening from microRNA regulation → p53-MDM2 targeted therapy → E3 ubiquitin ligase tumor suppression → CHIP clonal hematopoiesis.
Breakthrough Works 1. miR-1271 inhibits migration, invasion and epithelial-mesenchymal transition by targeting ZEB1 and TWIST1 in pancreatic cancer cells (2016) Description: This work is the first systematic demonstration that miR-1271 inhibits pancreatic cancer cell migration, invasion, and epithelial-mesenchymal transition by simultaneously targeting two core EMT transcription factors, ZEB1 and TWIST1, providing a dual-target miRNA regulatory framework for pancreatic cancer metastasis mechanisms.
Why This Wasn’t Done Earlier: Prior to 2016, high-throughput miRNA sequencing costs were still prohibitive, and systematic studies of miRNA regulatory networks in pancreatic cancer were limited. The mechanism by which ZEB1 and TWIST1 could be co-targeted by the same miRNA had not yet been uncovered. It was only with the widespread adoption of NGS technology and the maturation of bioinformatics tools that this dual-target regulatory axis could be systematically screened and validated.
Impact: With 62 citations, this became the most-cited paper of this researcher, advancing the establishment of multi-target miRNA regulatory strategies in pancreatic cancer metastasis research and serving as an important reference for subsequent EMT-related therapeutic target studies.
2. Antitumor immunity augments the therapeutic effects of p53 activation on acute myeloid leukemia (2019) Description: Published in Nature Communications, this study demonstrated that the orally active p53-MDM2 interaction inhibitor DS-5272 not only exerts antitumor effects through direct activation of p53 signaling in AML cells, but also synergistically enhances the host’s antitumor immune response, revealing a cooperative mechanism between targeted therapy and tumor immunity.
Why This Wasn’t Done Earlier: p53-MDM2 inhibitors had long been thought to act exclusively through cell-autonomous pathways, and the long-standing separation between tumor immunology and targeted drug research made this synergistic relationship difficult to discover. Around 2019, the cross-disciplinary integration of immuno-oncology and small-molecule targeted therapy became a major research focus, and the maturation of single-cell immune profiling technologies such as CyTOF enabled precise quantification of immune cell subset dynamics.
Impact: 42 citations, published in Nature Communications. This work expanded AML p53-targeted therapy research from a single cytotoxic paradigm to include immune regulation, providing a theoretical basis for subsequent clinical regimen designs combining immune checkpoint inhibitors.
3. Mutational inactivation of mTORC1 repressor gene DEPDC5 in human gastrointestinal stromal tumors (2019) Description: Published in PNAS, this study was the first to report that mutational inactivation of the mTORC1 negative regulator DEPDC5 is an important driver of GIST progression (rather than initiation), elucidating a new molecular mechanism underlying malignant progression of GISTs following KIT/PDGFRA mutations.
Why This Wasn’t Done Earlier: DEPDC5 had previously been studied primarily in neurological disorders such as focal epilepsy, and its connection to GIST had long gone undiscovered. Investigating GIST progression mechanisms required large-scale genomic sequencing data; the widespread clinical application of next-generation sequencing in tumor samples, combined with a systematic study design examining the ‘micro-GIST → advanced GIST’ evolutionary trajectory, were necessary prerequisites for this discovery.
Impact: 34 citations. This study provided a new explanation for the mechanisms of GIST progression following targeted therapy resistance, bringing the mTOR pathway into the pool of GIST therapeutic target candidates with direct clinical translational value.
4. E3 ligase MKRN3 is a tumor suppressor regulating PABPC1 ubiquitination in non–small cell lung cancer (2021) Description: Published in the Journal of Experimental Medicine, this study revealed that MKRN3 — a gene previously known for causing central precocious puberty — is a bona fide tumor suppressor in NSCLC. It regulates global protein synthesis by targeting PABPC1 for ubiquitination, uncovering a completely novel ‘E3 ligase–mRNA-binding protein–proteostasis’ tumor suppression axis.
Why This Wasn’t Done Earlier: MKRN3 had previously been recognized only as a regulator of pubertal development, with no known function in cancer. This discovery required the unique insight of cross-referencing clinical central precocious puberty genetics with cancer genomics databases, as well as mature ubiquitomics proteomics technology to identify PABPC1 as a substrate. This conceptual leap from ‘developmental disease gene → tumor suppressor’ depended on the completeness of large-scale cancer genomics databases such as TCGA.
Impact: 41 citations. This work provided a novel tumor suppression mechanism and potential therapeutic target for NSCLC, while establishing a new paradigm for studying the functional repurposing of developmental genes in cancer.
5. CHIP‐associated mutant ASXL1 in blood cells promotes solid tumor progression (2022) Description: This study revealed that blood cells harboring CHIP-associated ASXL1 mutations can promote solid tumor progression, establishing a causal link between clonal mutations in the hematopoietic system and non-hematopoietic tumors, and representing important evidence for the cross-system tumor influence of CHIP.
Why This Wasn’t Done Earlier: The recognition of CHIP as an independent risk factor itself only emerged between 2014 and 2017 through large-scale population cohort studies. Linking CHIP to solid tumor progression required the simultaneous availability of precise CHIP mouse models, solid tumor transplantation systems, and a deep understanding of bone marrow-derived cell function in the tumor microenvironment — technical and conceptual prerequisites that only converged around 2022.
Impact: 30 citations. This study extended CHIP research from cardiovascular disease and hematologic malignancy risk into the realm of solid tumors, with important clinical implications for risk stratification and treatment strategy formulation in elderly cancer patients.
Research Directions Targeted therapy and tumor immunity synergy mechanisms in AML/MDS (p53-MDM2, IMPDH inhibition) Functional roles of E3 ubiquitin ligases in tumors (STUB1/RUNX1, MKRN3/PABPC1) Molecular driver mechanisms and novel target discovery in solid tumors (GIST, NSCLC, pancreatic cancer) The role of microRNAs in cancer metastasis and EMT regulation Cross-system effects of clonal hematopoiesis (CHIP) on tumor progression Methodological Evolution The early phase (2005–2016) was dominated by single-molecule mechanism studies, employing the classic cell line experiment + overexpression/knockdown validation paradigm to investigate miRNA target gene regulation. From 2017 to 2019, research shifted toward integrative strategies, incorporating in vivo mouse models, drug intervention experiments, and immune cell profiling (CyTOF), achieving a leap from “mechanism discovery” to “therapeutic validation,” with the Nature Communications AML study as the flagship work. From 2020 to 2022, the approach was further upgraded to a two-tier strategy of systematic screening combined with deep mechanistic investigation: using CRISPR functional genomics screens or ubiquitomics mass spectrometry to identify targets (e.g., CDK1 screening, the MKRN3-PABPC1 axis), supplemented by patient sample database validation of clinical relevance, reflecting a significant increase in methodological sophistication.
Field Impact This researcher’s core contributions to molecular oncology are concentrated in three areas: (1) advancing the paradigm shift in AML targeted therapy from pure cytotoxic killing to a “targeted + immune synergy” model; (2) systematically expanding the understanding of E3 ubiquitin ligases as tumor suppressors in solid tumors, particularly through the discovery of MKRN3, which effectively bridges developmental biology and oncology; (3) providing new molecular targets and therapeutic strategies for refractory tumors such as GIST and NSCLC. Overall, this researcher is a steady contributor at the intersection of hematologic malignancies and solid tumors, with publications in high-impact journals including JEM, Nature Communications, PNAS, and EMBO Molecular Medicine. It should be noted, however, that an h-index of 12 is relatively low given the number of papers and top-journal publications, which may be partly due to data distortion from name aggregation — the actual academic impact of the individual researcher may be underestimated.

Xiongfei Yu — Researcher Analysis Report Analysis Mode: fast | Analysis Time: 2026-03-16T00:07:51
Rating: Early-Stage Researcher (28.3/100) Key Metrics Metric Value Affiliation Unknown h-index 6 Total Citations 248 Citations (Last 5 Years) 0 Total Papers 37 Top Venue Papers 0 Publication Span 1996 - 2019 Semantic Scholar 2112440444 Research Trajectory The academic trajectory of Xiongfei Yu exhibits a rare multi-phase cross-disciplinary development pattern, likely representing the aggregated works of multiple researchers sharing the same name. The first phase (1996–2004) centered on petroleum engineering in China, focusing on gas condensate prediction in gas reservoirs, CO₂ injection for enhanced oil recovery (EOR), and reservoir screening for gas injection — with a distinctly Chinese oilfield (Liaohe, Daqing, etc.) engineering background. The second phase (2005–2011) saw a dramatic shift toward geotechnical and civil engineering, with publications on 3D finite element analysis of dam stability, levee systems in Louisiana, and pile foundation testing in Georgia — work characteristic of North American engineering practice that bears almost no connection to petroleum engineering. The third phase (2017–2019) marks a return to the petroleum domain, but at a higher research level: rock physics and unconventional reservoir mechanics — focused on compaction and sand production effects on fluid flow in unconsolidated sandstones, and NMR spontaneous imbibition in tight sandstones — mirroring the wave of large-scale shale/tight oil development in China. The most-cited paper (2019, 108 citations) represents the apex of this researcher’s academic impact.
Landmark Works 1. New insights into spontaneous imbibition in tight oil sandstones with NMR (2019) Description: Using nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) technology, this work systematically elucidated the microscopic mechanisms and quantitative patterns of capillary-driven spontaneous imbibition in tight sandstone reservoirs, providing experimental evidence for the oil production mechanism during post-fracturing shut-in periods, and directly guiding the optimization of fracturing fluid flowback and shut-in schedules in tight oil reservoirs.
Why it wasn’t done before: Pore throats in tight sandstones are extremely small (nanoscale), and conventional mercury intrusion and core experiments could not characterize fluid distribution and migration in situ and dynamically. The maturation of NMR core analysis technology and the widespread availability of high-field instruments provided the prerequisite for such research. Furthermore, China’s large-scale commercial development of shale/tight oil (post-2015) created urgent engineering demand, which in turn secured experimental funding and sample access.
Impact: With 108 citations, this paper has become an important reference in the study of spontaneous imbibition in tight reservoirs, driving rapid development of the “shut-in optimization — capillary-driven oil recovery” engineering direction within China’s unconventional oil and gas sector.
2. Stability analyses of dam abutments by 3D elasto-plastic finite-element method: A case study of Houhe gravity-arch dam in China (2005) Description: Using the Houhe gravity-arch dam in China as a case study, this work systematically analyzed dam abutment stability using a 3D elasto-plastic finite element method, providing a numerical simulation framework applicable to real engineering validation. With 39 citations, it was the most impactful work of this phase.
Why it wasn’t done before: 3D elasto-plastic finite element models require significant computational resources, and engineering-grade 3D rock mass simulation was not yet widespread in China before 2005. At the same time, China’s large-scale hydraulic engineering construction (following the Three Gorges Project) provided the case studies and engineering demand that made this work possible.
Impact: It established a methodological template for seismic analysis and dam abutment stability assessment in Chinese gravity-arch dams, and continues to receive sustained citations in the hydraulic and geotechnical engineering literature.
3. Fluid flow with compaction and sand production in unconsolidated sandstone reservoir (2018) Description: Through single-phase displacement experiments, this work quantitatively measured the coupled effects of compaction and sand production on fluid flow behavior in unconsolidated sandstones, establishing a fluid flow model that accounts for reservoir damage, and providing experimental evidence for predicting well productivity decline.
Why it wasn’t done before: Specialized high-pressure core holders capable of simultaneously controlling compaction degree and sand production are difficult to fabricate; experimental operations on unconsolidated sandstones (sample preparation and encapsulation) are technically demanding, and relevant experimental data were previously very scarce.
Impact: With 23 citations, it provides an experimental foundation for productivity evaluation and sand control design in heavy oil and loose sandstone reservoirs (such as Liaohe Oilfield).
4. The variation mechanism of petrophysical properties and the effect of compaction on the relative permeability of an unconsolidated sandstone reservoir (2017) Description: This work systematically studied the variation of petrophysical parameters (porosity, permeability) with compaction in unconsolidated sandstone reservoirs, particularly quantifying the effect of compaction on gas-water relative permeability curves, and revealing the mechanism behind the decline in flow capacity during production.
Why it wasn’t done before: Relative permeability experiments are typically conducted on consolidated cores; combined dynamic compaction and two-phase flow experiments on unconsolidated sandstones present significant technical and equipment challenges, and such data have long been lacking.
Impact: With 18 citations, it fills a gap in compaction-flow coupling research for unconsolidated sandstones and provides practical reference for development planning in offshore heavy oil and loose sandstone reservoirs.
5. A SCREENING CANDIDATE RESERVOIR FOR GAS INJECTION WAY BASED ON CHARACTERISTIC PARAMETER’S COMPREHENSIVE WEIGHT OPTIMIZATION METHOD (2004) Description: This work proposed a reservoir screening method for gas injection candidates based on comprehensive weight optimization of characteristic parameters, providing a quantitative decision-making framework for reservoir selection in CO₂/N₂/natural gas miscible flooding — a systematic methodological contribution to early EOR engineering practice.
Why it wasn’t done before: Early reservoir screening largely relied on empirical judgment based on single parameters, lacking a multi-parameter quantitative evaluation methodology. Meanwhile, China’s large-scale CO₂-EOR pilot tests (early 2000s) provided the accumulated data needed to refine screening criteria.
Impact: With 6 citations, it provides a reusable systematic screening framework for the preliminary assessment of gas injection EOR projects in Chinese oilfields.
Research Areas Rock physics of tight and unconsolidated sandstone reservoirs (compaction, sand production, relative permeability, NMR spontaneous imbibition) Gas injection enhanced oil recovery (CO₂/N₂/natural gas miscible flooding, reservoir screening and evaluation) Geotechnical and civil engineering (dam abutment stability finite element analysis, geophysical exploration, flood levee systems) Fluid flow mechanisms in unconventional reservoirs and production performance prediction Methodological Evolution In the early phase (1996–2004), research was primarily based on engineering empirical methods, including fuzzy analytical hierarchy process (FAHP), parameter optimization screening, and semi-empirical predictive models — emphasizing decision-support tools for oilfield engineering practice with limited experimental validation. The middle phase (2005–2011) shifted methodologically toward numerical simulation (3D elasto-plastic finite element) and geophysical field testing (CPTu, seismic waves, borehole geophysics), reflecting a trend toward greater precision in geotechnical engineering. The later phase (2017–2019) completed the transition from macro-engineering to micro-mechanism research: centered on high-pressure core physical experiments (single-phase/two-phase displacement, compaction loading apparatus) combined with NMR pore-scale characterization, supplemented by numerical simulation and theoretical modeling — representing the mainstream paradigm of contemporary reservoir physics research.
Field Impact Overall, the academic impact of Xiongfei Yu (or the group of researchers sharing this name) exhibits a pattern of “late-stage concentrated outburst”: early and middle-phase papers attracted scattered citations (maximum 39 per paper), whereas three rock physics papers published in just three years (2017–2019) collectively contributed approximately 149 citations — accounting for over 60% of the total 248 citations — with the 2019 NMR spontaneous imbibition paper (108 citations) having become a highly cited work in tight sandstone flow research. This pattern suggests that the researcher(s) identified a critical research opportunity at the right moment — during the rise of unconventional reservoirs — leveraging refined laboratory characterization capabilities to make specific, quantifiable academic contributions to China’s shale/tight oil development technology. However, an h-index of only 6 and anomalous near-5-year citation statistics suggest potential author disambiguation issues in the database; it is recommended to cross-verify using affiliation information.

Rating: Active Researcher (30.3/100) Basic Metrics Metric Value Institution Unknown h-index 10 Total Citations 375 Citations (Last 5 Years) 0 Total Papers 101 Top-Conference Papers 0 Publication Years 1990 - 2020 Semantic Scholar 9460406 Research Trajectory Warning: This “Yanyan Chen” academic profile has a severe Name Disambiguation Failure — Semantic Scholar has very likely merged dozens of researchers sharing the name “Yanyan Chen” into a single profile. The paper list makes this unambiguously clear: superconducting thin films (1990), intelligent transportation (2003), thermoacoustic refrigeration (2007), schistosomiasis epidemiology (2009), Moso bamboo invasion ecology (2013), thermoacoustic engines (2014), shale gas geology (2016), topological quantum field theory (2023) — these fields share no common knowledge base and could not possibly originate from a single person. The profile’s h-index of 10 and 375 total citations across 101 papers is also inconsistent with the development trajectory of any single real researcher. The analysis below traces the most citation-influential research threads within this merged profile, not the academic trajectory of any single scholar.
Landmark Works 1. Shale Gas Formation and Occurrence in China: An Overview of the Current Status and Future Potential (2016) Description: A systematic review of shale gas geological origins, reservoir characteristics, and development potential in China. With 56 citations, it is the most-cited single paper in this profile and serves as an important reference in China’s shale gas industrial policy and resource assessment.
Why it wasn’t possible before: Prior to 2016, China lacked sufficient shale gas exploration data, and systematic geological evaluation of regions such as the Sichuan Basin was still in its early stages. The concurrent U.S. shale gas revolution provided a comparable technical framework, making comparative research feasible.
Impact: Provided a systematic reference for domestic shale gas resource assessment and supported energy policy discussions on shale gas development pathways.
2. Plant species diversity and dynamics in forests invaded by Moso bamboo (Phyllostachys edulis) in Tianmu Mountain Nature Reserve (2013) Description: A study of the effects of Moso bamboo expansion on forest plant diversity in Tianmu Mountain Nature Reserve. Cited 53 times, it is a key empirical study on bamboo spread mechanisms in the field of biological invasion ecology.
Why it wasn’t possible before: Long-term fixed-plot monitoring data were required, and Tianmu Mountain’s vegetation monitoring system only matured in the late 2000s. Meanwhile, the ecological threat of Moso bamboo expansion only began receiving systematic attention from Chinese ecologists in the early 2010s.
Impact: Provided ecological evidence for subtropical forest conservation and Moso bamboo management policy in China, and helped advance the research agenda on the effects of bamboo expansion on biodiversity.
3. Heat transfer characteristics of oscillating flow regenerator filled with circular tubes or parallel plates (2007) Description: An investigation of the heat transfer characteristics of oscillating flow regenerators — a core component of thermoacoustic systems. Cited 35 times, this is a foundational experimental study in thermoacoustic refrigeration and cryogenic engineering.
Why it wasn’t possible before: The heat transfer mechanism in oscillating flow is complex, and early measurement instruments lacked the precision to distinguish heat transfer differences between circular tube and parallel plate geometries. The maturation of thermoacoustic experimental platforms in the early 2000s made precise parametric studies possible.
Impact: Provided experimental evidence for the optimized design of regenerators in thermoacoustic refrigerators and engines. The subsequent series of thermoacoustic engine papers from 2014–2015 (cited 27–30 times) all build upon this work.
4. Study of a liquid-piston traveling-wave thermoacoustic heat engine with different working gases (2014) Description: A study of the performance of a traveling-wave thermoacoustic heat engine using different working gases. Cited 30 times, this represents systematic experimental exploration of liquid-piston configurations in the field of thermoacoustic energy conversion.
Why it wasn’t possible before: Sealing and dynamic stability issues in liquid-piston thermoacoustic systems were difficult to address in early engineering implementations. Traveling-wave thermoacoustic theory matured gradually after Backhaus & Swift (1999), and experimental validation work only proliferated in the 2010s.
Impact: Advanced the transition of thermoacoustic engines from laboratory concepts to engineering applications, particularly for potential use in low-grade waste heat recovery.
5. An Evaluating Method of Public Transit Accessibility for Urban Areas Based on GIS (2016) Description: Proposes a GIS-based method for evaluating public transit accessibility in urban areas (APTA concept). Cited 21 times, it integrates passenger behavioral psychology and service area quantification into urban transportation planning.
Why it wasn’t possible before: Large-scale GIS spatial analysis was computationally expensive before the 2010s. The widespread adoption of mobile internet and transit IC card data made behavioral parameter calibration feasible, enabling accessibility models to move from theory to practical implementation.
Impact: Provided a methodological tool for the quantitative evaluation of urban public transit planning, with notable reference value in domestic urban transportation research communities.
Research Directions Thermoacoustic systems and cryogenic engineering (oscillating flow regenerators, traveling-wave thermoacoustic engines, heat pumps) Urban transportation planning and intelligent transportation systems (GIS accessibility, OD data, network reliability) Public health and epidemiology (schistosomiasis, thyroid function, diabetes prevention) Ecology and resource geology (bamboo invasion, shale gas resource assessment) Topological quantum field theory (topological order, Levin-Wen model) — likely a distinct researcher active in recent years Methodological Evolution Because this profile aggregates multiple researchers, it is impossible to describe the methodological evolution of a single scholar. Looking within each research thread: the thermoacoustic thread (2007→2014→2015) follows a path from fundamental heat transfer experiments → system integration experiments → engineering with linear compressor drives; the transportation thread (2002→2007→2016→2017) progresses from early GIS+genetic-algorithm heuristics toward quantitative evaluation based on behavioral psychology models; the topological field theory papers appearing from 2023–2025 (quantum double models, Levin-Wen models, instantons on Young diagrams) represent an entirely different theoretical physics researcher with no connection to any other work in this profile.
Field Impact This profile does not represent the field impact of any single researcher. As a merged profile, its 375 total citations are scattered across at least 5–6 completely different academic disciplines, with no single field reaching a significant impact threshold (h-index = 10). To assess the impact of any individual real researcher within this profile, disambiguation must first be performed using institutional affiliation, co-author networks, and email domains. Recommendation: Any academic evaluation of this profile must begin with manual disambiguation; directly using this aggregated data will lead to severely misleading conclusions.

Yingying Huang — Researcher Analysis Report Analysis Mode: fast | Analysis Time: 2026-03-16T00:06:23
Rating: Active Researcher (33.5/100) Basic Metrics Metric Value Affiliation Unknown h-index 6 Total Citations 86 Citations (Last 5 Years) 0 Total Papers 6 Top Conference Papers 1 Publication Period 2016 - 2020 Semantic Scholar 2145496035 Research Trajectory Based on the publication list, this “Yingying Huang” is most likely the result of merged data from multiple researchers sharing the same name, rather than a single researcher’s academic trajectory. Publications span chemical biology (DNA-small molecule interactions, natural products), plasma physics (GS reconstruction), condensed matter physics (VO₂ phase transitions), surface physics, machine learning/BCI, nanosensors, autonomous driving, and geophysical forward modeling — these fields have virtually no overlap in methodology or research community, and it is exceedingly rare for a single researcher to cross so many primary disciplines within such a short career span.
Treating this as a single researcher’s trajectory: the early period (2016–2017) focused on medicinal chemistry, studying the interaction between naphthalimide-polyamine conjugates and DNA through spectroscopy and molecular docking, with 3 related publications (50 total citations, forming the backbone of the h-index). In 2020, the focus shifted to natural products chemistry, with novel bioactive compounds (PPAPs) isolated from endophytic fungi and Hypericum plants, along with a COX-2 inhibitor study. From 2023 onward, entirely new directions appeared — EEG representation learning, piezoelectric sensors, BCI-assisted drawing, LLM-aided driving perception, and neural operator geophysical modeling — representing a complete break from earlier work.
The most reasonable explanation is that databases such as Semantic Scholar have merged publications from multiple namesake authors, creating clear contradictions between the reported h-index (6), total citations (86), and publication period (2016–2020) versus the actual paper list (which extends to 2025). The “Citations (Last 5 Years): 0” figure is also at odds with the existence of active new publications in 2025.
Breakthrough Works 1. Spectroscopic and molecular modeling methods to study the interaction between naphthalimide-polyamine conjugates and DNA (2016) Description: Systematically investigated the interaction between naphthalimide-polyamine conjugates (compounds 1–7) and herring sperm DNA under physiological pH conditions, combining UV/fluorescence spectroscopy with molecular docking to elucidate how polyamine side-chain structure governs binding mode.
Why It Wasn’t Done Before: No systematic workflow existed for combining spectroscopic experimental data with in silico molecular docking, making it difficult to simultaneously quantify — from both experimental and theoretical perspectives — how side-chain length contributes to intercalation versus groove-binding modes.
Impact: Cited 34 times, making it the most-cited paper in this profile. It provided an experiment-computation joint analysis framework for structure optimization of naphthalimide derivatives as potential anticancer agents, advancing the subsequent design of polyamine-intercalator conjugate systems.
2. New bioactive secondary metabolites from the Anoectochilus roxburghii endophytic fungus Aspergillus versicolor (2020) Description: Isolated and identified 6 new compounds from the endophytic fungus Aspergillus versicolor of Anoectochilus roxburghii, including two novel isochroman lactones (versicoisochromanes A/B), two benzolactones, and a new sterol, with absolute configurations determined by NMR and ECD.
Why It Wasn’t Done Before: Secondary metabolite studies on endophytic fungi of Anoectochilus roxburghii were exceedingly rare, and the fungus’s unique chemical environment produced rare scaffolds. The widespread availability of high-resolution mass spectrometry and ECD calculations has significantly lowered the barrier to structural elucidation of trace novel compounds.
Impact: Cited 16 times. Expanded the known chemical diversity of the genus Aspergillus, with the newly discovered scaffolds contributing novel templates to the natural products library.
3. Hypersonins A-D, Polycyclic Polyprenylated Acylphloroglucinols with a 1,2-seco-Homoadamantane Architecture from Hypericum wilsonii (2020) Description: Isolated 4 polycyclic polyprenylated acylphloroglucinols (PPAPs) featuring a novel 1,2-seco-homoadamantane skeleton from Hypericum wilsonii, with the new scaffold type confirmed through comprehensive spectroscopic data, X-ray crystallography, and ECD calculations.
Why It Wasn’t Done Before: Structural elucidation of PPAPs relies on high-field NMR and computation-assisted absolute configuration determination (TDDFT/ECD), tools that only became mature and widely accessible in recent years. Deeper chemosystematic studies of the Hypericum genus also contributed to uncovering such rare skeletal architectures.
Impact: Cited 13 times. Reported the first naturally occurring PPAPs featuring a bicyclo[4.3.1]decane-3-methoxycarbonyl architecture, contributing a new scaffold type to the study of structural diversity in this compound family.
4. A Knowledge-Driven Cross-view Contrastive Learning for EEG Representation (2023) Description: Proposed a cross-view contrastive learning framework incorporating neurophysiological prior knowledge for unsupervised/self-supervised EEG representation learning, addressing the bottleneck of scarce labeled EEG data.
Why It Wasn’t Done Before: The challenge in EEG contrastive learning lies in constructing meaningful positive/negative sample pairs. Prior methods lacked mechanisms for systematically embedding neuroscientific domain knowledge (e.g., frequency bands, brain region topology) into contrastive learning frameworks, and the rise of large-scale pretraining paradigms is what made unsupervised EEG representation learning a viable direction.
Impact: This work (if by the same person) marks a significant shift in research direction, combining neuroscientific domain knowledge with modern self-supervised learning to offer new approaches for BCI applications constrained by limited labeled data.
Research Areas Medicinal chemistry and DNA interactions (naphthalimide/anthracene-polyamine conjugates, spectroscopy + molecular docking) Natural products chemistry (isolation, identification, and bioactivity evaluation of secondary metabolites from endophytic fungi and Hypericum plants) Brain-computer interfaces and EEG signal processing (contrastive learning, visual BCI, drawing assistance) Materials and sensors (VO₂ phase transitions, nanoscale piezoelectric sensors, contact angle physics) AI-assisted scientific computing (geophysical forward modeling, LLM-aided driving perception) Methodological Evolution Viewed as a single-researcher trajectory: the early period (2016–2017) was methodologically centered on experimental spectroscopy (UV/vis, fluorescence) combined with molecular docking, representing a traditional medicinal chemistry paradigm. In 2020, the approach shifted to the standard isolation-and-identification workflow of natural products chemistry (chromatographic separation, NMR structure elucidation, ECD-based absolute configuration determination, in vitro bioactivity evaluation). Work appearing from 2023 onward pivots entirely to data-driven deep learning methods (contrastive learning, neural operators, large language models), with virtually no continuity in toolchain or knowledge base from either of the two earlier phases.
This methodological discontinuity further supports the “multiple namesakes” hypothesis — a genuine single researcher typically retains some degree of methodological or domain continuity, with transitional work even when changing directions, whereas the breaks seen here are abrupt.
Field Impact Since this researcher profile is most likely an aggregation of data from multiple namesake scholars, it is difficult to accurately assess the field impact of any “single researcher.” Based on the verifiable early publications, there is a meaningful contribution to structure-activity relationship studies on naphthalimide-class DNA intercalators (up to 34 citations), and a substantive natural products chemistry contribution in the discovery of novel PPAP scaffolds from Hypericum. Overall, with an h-index of 6 and 86 total citations, this profile represents a typical researcher at the subdisciplinary level, not yet at the scale of foundational influence. When citing or reviewing work, it is advisable to first confirm via ORCID or institutional profiles which specific “Yingying Huang” corresponds to which set of publications before making precise academic evaluations.
